surface: Design system & token coherence (whole app) — globals.css, cockpit/tokens.ts, tailwind.config.ts
feelVerdict: Up close this reads like one tasteful, warm-editorial system — but it is actually four design systems wearing a trench coat (globals HSL vars, hardcoded-hex 'T' cockpit, Mercury, v6 landing), and they quietly disagree on the brand's own colors. The taste is real and the intent is premium; the foundation is not coherent or dark-safe enough to feel trustworthy under pressure. The most dangerous part is a shipped Light/Dark/System toggle that breaks ~70% of surfaces the moment anyone touches it.

strengths.title: Genuinely tasteful, coherent intent — warm-editorial restraint is the right instinct
strengths.detail: The light-mode palette is calm and premium: warm-white --background hsl(45 25% 98%), a single operator-amber action color, agent hues reserved for identity, editorial serif/mono. This is the correct aesthetic bet for a time-poor operator trusting AI with money, and it mostly reads as one calm surface in light mode.

strengths.title: Agent-identity-only discipline is explicitly encoded, not just hoped for
strengths.detail: The rule 'agent hue = identity only, never on action buttons' is written into the tokens and the Tailwind config, and the cockpit config files repeat it. That single-action-color discipline is exactly what keeps the UI from feeling like a noisy dashboard.
strengths.evidence: globals.css:61 ('Per-agent IDENTITY ONLY — never on action buttons'); tailwind.config.ts:73; mira-config.ts:6-7

strengths.title: A clean accent abstraction already exists and one config proves the right pattern
strengths.detail: AccentTokens {base,deep,soft,paper} gives every agent a consistent shape, and mira-config.ts already expresses it as hsl(var(--agent-mira)) rather than hex — proving the cockpit CAN be CSS-var-backed and themeable. The migration path to one system is already half-demonstrated in the codebase.

strengths.title: Motion + reduced-motion are tokenized and respected
strengths.detail: Durations/eases are tokens (--duration-*, --ease-*) and there are TWO prefers-reduced-motion guards plus token-driven onboarding transitions — a real accessibility/polish signal most dashboards skip.

issues.title: Shipped Light/Dark/System toggle breaks ~70% of surfaces — and can auto-trigger with no user action
issues.detail: use-theme.ts toggles .dark on documentElement, exposed as a real 3-way control in Settings → Account. In 'system' mode it auto-applies dark when the OS prefers it, so dark can activate on first load with zero user action on a dark-OS phone. But only 40 of 145 :root tokens have a .dark value. Every editorial token (--ink, --cream, --hair, --serif, --ink-2/3/4), every agent identity token, every shadow, the whole --mercury-* register, and the entire hardcoded-hex 'T' cockpit family have NO dark value. Home, the Mira creative desk, and every cockpit render black-ink/warm-cream on a dark canvas — illegible. A medspa owner who flips to Dark (or just has a dark phone) loses trust instantly.
issues.evidence: use-theme.ts:14-18 (classList.toggle('dark'); system→prefers-color-scheme); settings/account/page.tsx:184-198 (Light/Dark/System buttons); globals.css .dark 231-289 covers ~40 tokens; --ink/--cream/--hair/--shadow-*/--mercury-*/--agent-* absent from .dark

issues.title: The hex 'T' cockpit system is structurally un-themeable — a permanent second design system
issues.detail: tokens.ts is 18 literal hex/rgba values with zero var() references, rendered through ~60 inline style={{ color: T.ink }} bindings across the cockpit/Mira family. Inline styles + literal hex are invisible to the CSS cascade: .dark can't override them, a rebrand can't retarget them, they can never join the HSL token system. The two systems literally collide in inbox-agent-avatar.tsx, which imports hex-derived ALEX/RILEY accents AND hand-defines a fresh #4A3A66 Mira that ignores the live --agent-mira-deep token.
issues.evidence: tokens.ts:1-18 (all literal hex/rgba); ~60 inline style={{...T.x}} in src/components/cockpit; inbox-agent-avatar.tsx:24-27 (#4A3A66 vs globals --agent-mira-deep 256 31% 45%)

issues.title: Each agent's identity color is defined 2–3 times with conflicting values
issues.detail: The same brand hue renders different shades depending on the surface. Alex coral: globals --agent-alex hsl(14 70% 58%) vs tailwind v6.coral hsl(14 75% 55%) vs alex-config #E07A53 (≈hsl(14 70% 60%)) — and the alex-config comment falsely claims it 'matches --agent-alex hsl(14 70% 58%)'. Mira deep: token --agent-mira-deep 256 31% 45% vs the inbox avatar's hardcoded #4A3A66 (≈261 27% 31%). Brand color drifts between the Home verdict hero, the cockpit card, and the inbox avatar — the opposite of byte-perfect Linear/Stripe identity.
issues.evidence: globals.css:62 (--agent-alex); tailwind.config.ts:93 (v6.coral); alex-config.ts:10 (#E07A53 + inaccurate 'matches' comment); globals.css:213 vs inbox-agent-avatar.tsx:26 (#4A3A66)

issues.title: No enforced spacing scale — tokens are dead, real spacing is free-hand px
issues.detail: The editorial spacing tokens (--space-page-x/y, --space-section, --space-block) have ZERO consumers outside globals.css — dead code. Actual spacing lives as raw px in CSS modules with no rhythm: 7px, 9px, 11px, 14px, 18px, 22px, 28px all appear next to 8/16/24, so it's neither a 4pt nor 8pt grid. Every component picks its own numbers, which reads as subtly 'off' spacing the eye can't name but feels uncrafted.
issues.evidence: globals.css:126-129 (--space-* defined) with 0 var(--space-*) consumers outside globals.css; module px histogram shows 7/9/11/14/18/22px in active use

issues.title: Elevation is fragmented across ~35 ad-hoc shadows with four different shadow base colors
issues.detail: Three named --shadow-* tokens exist, but elevation is actually ~20 distinct ad-hoc box-shadows in CSS modules PLUS ~15 Tailwind shadow-[...] arbitrary values in TSX — and they disagree on the shadow color itself: rgba(40,30,20), rgba(14,12,10), hsl(20 12% 4%), and hsl(20 30% 30%) are all used. There's no z-index→elevation ladder. A card on Home, a sheet on Inbox, and a popover in the cockpit cast different-colored, different-spread shadows, so the product never settles into one light source — a strong subliminal 'unpolished' cue.
issues.evidence: globals.css:218-220 (3 --shadow-* tokens) vs ~20 ad-hoc module shadows + ~15 Tailwind shadow-[...]; bases diverge: rgba(40,30,20), rgba(14,12,10), hsl(20 12% 4%), hsl(20 30% 30%)

issues.title: globals.css mixes two value conventions and collides on the name 'accent' — a self-documented footgun
issues.detail: ~50 tokens are raw HSL triples meant to be wrapped (--accent: 40 18% 91% → hsl(var(--accent))) and ~52 are complete values used bare (--editorial-accent: hsl(20 90% 55%), --sw-accent: #A07850). The word 'accent' means both encodings: --accent (raw) vs --editorial-accent/--mercury-accent/--char-accent (complete). The file's own comment admits it. Any engineer will eventually write hsl(var(--editorial-accent)) or bare var(--accent) and get an invalid/transparent color — a known class of bug per the repo's own collision notes.
issues.evidence: globals.css:193-199 (self-documented convention comment); --accent:40 18% 91% (raw, :30) vs --editorial-accent:hsl(20 90% 55%) (complete, :199); 50 raw-triple vs 52 complete-value tokens in :root

issues.title: Radius scale has a redundant rung and is bypassed by hardcoded corners
issues.detail: --radius (DEFAULT) and --radius-md are both 0.5rem, a duplicate step, so the ladder is effectively sm/8/lg/xl with a dead rung. Worse, corner radii are also hardcoded in the cockpit/editorial CSS (border-radius:6px on .win/.tile, inline borderRadius:8 in Mira/inbox), independent of the --radius-* tokens — so rounding is not driven by one scale.
issues.evidence: globals.css:77-81 (--radius 0.5rem == --radius-md 0.5rem); :846/:1071 (.win/.tile 6px); inbox/mira inline borderRadius:8

issues.title: Type system is larger than the config knows, with no size scale
issues.detail: Tailwind exposes only sans/display/mono, but the real type system has ~6 roles wired in layout.tsx and consumed via raw var() (--serif Source Serif 4, --mono-editorial, --font-home-sans Hanken, --font-home-serif Newsreader). So font choices can't be made consistently through one mechanism. There's also no type-scale token — sizes are raw px everywhere (greeting-prose 32→48→60, hero-num 54→88→140).
issues.evidence: tailwind.config.ts:109-113 (only sans/display/mono) vs globals.css:183-224 (--serif/--mono/--font-home-*); no --font-size-* tokens; raw px sizes :744-1027

opportunities.title: Make the cockpit CSS-var-backed (merge systems 1 & 2 with no redesign)
opportunities.detail: Rewrite tokens.ts T and the *_ACCENT constants to reference CSS vars (hsl(var(--ink)), hsl(var(--agent-alex))) instead of literal hex — mira-config.ts already proves the pattern. Components barely change (they already read T.x / accent.base), but the cockpit instantly becomes dark-capable and rebrandable, and the Alex-coral 3-way drift collapses to one source. Single highest-leverage fix.

opportunities.title: Resolve the dark-mode liability before it ships harm
opportunities.detail: Either complete the .dark block for all 145 tokens (editorial + agent + shadow + mercury + a CSS-var cockpit), or hide Dark/System in Settings and force light until the full dark palette exists. A visible toggle that breaks Home, Mira, and every cockpit is worse than no toggle.

opportunities.title: Collapse each agent hue to one source + add a drift-guard test
opportunities.detail: Promote one ramp per agent in globals.css (the --agent-*-deep/-tint tokens already exist) and delete #E07A53, v6.coral, and the #4A3A66 Mira. Add a tokens.test.ts assertion (the repo already has src/app/__tests__/tokens.test.ts) that no agent hex lives outside globals.css, so drift can't reappear.

opportunities.title: Ship a real elevation ladder + enforced spacing scale
opportunities.detail: Define --shadow-1..5 (resting/raised/overlay/modal/popover) with ONE base color, map z-index to it, and replace the ~35 ad-hoc/arbitrary shadows. Pair with a fixed spacing scale (4/8/12/16/24/32/48/64) actually applied — expose --space-* via Tailwind spacing so it's enforced — and retire the dead tokens. This is the 'feels crafted' layer.

opportunities.title: Unify globals.css on ONE value convention and de-collide 'accent'
opportunities.detail: Pick raw-HSL-triple everywhere (or complete-value everywhere) and rename --accent vs --editorial-accent/--mercury-accent so one word never means two encodings. Removes the file's self-documented invalid-color footgun and makes the token file safe to extend.

topMoves: Convert the hex 'T' object + all *_ACCENT constants (tokens.ts, alex-config, riley-config, inbox-agent-avatar) to reference CSS vars instead of literal hex — mira-config.ts already proves the pattern. This merges the two systems, unblocks dark mode for the cockpit, and kills the Alex-coral 3-way drift in one move.

Resolve the dark-mode liability: either complete the .dark block for all 145 tokens (editorial + agent + shadow + mercury + CSS-var cockpit), or hide the Dark/System toggle in Settings → Account until the full dark palette exists. Do not ship a toggle that breaks Home, Mira, and every cockpit.

Collapse each agent identity hue to ONE definition in globals.css and delete the conflicting copies (#E07A53, v6.coral, #4A3A66); add a tokens.test.ts guard asserting no agent hex lives outside globals.css so drift can't return.

Build a real elevation ladder (--shadow-1..5, one base color, z-index-mapped) and enforce a fixed spacing scale, replacing the ~35 ad-hoc/arbitrary shadows and the free-hand 7/9/11/14/18/22px module spacing; retire the dead --space-* tokens.

Unify globals.css on ONE value convention (raw-HSL-triple everywhere) and rename the colliding --accent vs --editorial-accent/--mercury-accent tokens so the same word never means two encodings; this removes the file's self-documented invalid-color footgun.

feelVerdict: When you're inside the new Home/Inbox register it feels genuinely editorial and premium — Newsreader on an optical-size axis, lowercase italic serif headers, a drop-capped "weeknote" that reads like a letter from a colleague. But the moment you cross into an agent cockpit (Alex/Riley/Mira KPI strips, approval cards), the typography collapses into a generic Inter + hardcoded-pixel admin tool with no serif, jittery proportional numbers, and ASCII quotes — so the same agent literally speaks in two different voices, and the most trust-critical screens (where money numbers and approvals live) are the LEAST crafted.

strengths.title: The Home 'weeknote' is best-in-class editorial typography
strengths.detail: Newsreader serif on an optical-size axis with a real drop cap (opsz 72, italic, float left), an em-dash sign-off with a monogram, tabular-figure inline numbers, and 'an employee email, not a dashboard' as the literal design intent. This is the kind of type craft Stripe/Mercury ship — it makes Alex feel like a person, not a widget.

strengths.title: Newsreader optical-size axis is wired correctly and used with intent
strengths.detail: The variable opsz axis is loaded properly (axes:['opsz'] in layout.tsx) and consumed at the right sizes everywhere: opsz 72 for the 56px verdict line, opsz 24 for 22px h2s, opsz 18 for 19px body, opsz 18 for small avatar monograms. Display text gets tighter/higher-contrast cuts and small text gets sturdier cuts — exactly what optical sizing is for. Almost no team bothers.

strengths.title: Verdict line + section headers establish a confident editorial hierarchy
strengths.detail: The Home verdict is a 36→56px Newsreader line with -0.022em tracking, text-wrap:balance, and a 22ch measure; section headers are lowercase italic serif h2s with a hairline baseline rule. The mono eyebrow + serif headline + mono proof-pip stack is a coherent three-tier rhythm that reads top-down at a glance.

strengths.title: Inbox swipe card carries the editorial voice into the decision feed
strengths.detail: The approval card's agent name is Newsreader italic at opsz 20 in the agent's deep identity hue, the title is balanced serif at opsz 24, risk pills and SLA use mono — the high-traffic decision surface (where most operator time goes) is held to the editorial bar, not reskinned as a list row.

strengths.title: Results respects tabular figures rigorously for ROI numbers
strengths.detail: Every metric on Results uses font-variant-numeric:tabular-nums (28 occurrences) so ROI/CAC/spend numbers stay column-aligned and don't shimmer as they update — the right call for the screen where operators check whether their money worked.

issues.title: The agent cockpits use a totally different, un-editorial type system — same agent, two voices
issues.detail: The cockpit family (KPI strip, KPI tile, approval card, identity header, Mira desk) has NO typography tokens at all — tokens.ts exports only colors. Every size/weight/family is a hardcoded inline magic number (fontSize: 10, 26, 36; fontWeight: 700; fontFamily: 'JetBrains Mono' string-literals). Crucially there is NO serif anywhere: the agent's name renders in Inter (identity.tsx:85) and the approval title in Inter 600 (approval-card.tsx:124), whereas the SAME agent in Home/Inbox speaks in Newsreader italic serif. An operator who taps from Inbox into Alex's cockpit watches Alex's typographic personality evaporate. This is the single biggest editorial-consistency break in the app.
issues.evidence: src/components/cockpit/tokens.ts:1-18 (colors only, zero type); src/components/cockpit/identity.tsx:84-89; src/components/cockpit/approval-card.tsx:124-135; src/components/cockpit/kpi-tile.tsx:27-33

issues.title: data-tabular is a no-op — KPI/ROI metric figures jitter in proportional Inter
issues.detail: kpi-tile.tsx and roi-bar.tsx tag their value spans with data-tabular intending tabular figures, but there is NO global [data-tabular] CSS rule anywhere, and valueStyleBase sets neither fontVariantNumeric nor a mono/serif family. So the cockpit's headline numbers render in Inter's PROPORTIONAL figures — digit widths differ, and live-updating values (booked count, CPB, ROI) visibly shift horizontally. On the screens whose entire job is 'is the number good?', the numbers are the least stable.
issues.evidence: src/components/cockpit/kpi-tile.tsx:27-33,40,71 (data-tabular set, no tnum); grep '[data-tabular]' in *.css → no match

issues.title: Three different fonts for 'the big number that matters' across surfaces
issues.detail: The hero metric has no canonical typeface: Mercury home renders it in Source Serif at 54–140px (.hero-num), Results renders it in JetBrains Mono at 28–36px (.heroStatNum), and the cockpit renders it in Inter at 26px (kpi-tile valueStyleBase). A medspa owner glancing between Home, Results, and an agent cockpit sees their KPIs in serif, mono, and sans respectively — there's no learned 'this is a Switchboard number' shape, which quietly erodes the feeling of one coherent product.
issues.evidence: src/app/globals.css:910-916 (.hero-num serif); src/components/results/results.module.css:99-106 (.heroStatNum mono); src/components/cockpit/kpi-tile.tsx:27-33 (Inter)

issues.title: Straight ASCII quotes on the cockpit testimonial pull-quote
issues.detail: The approval card wraps a customer quote in literal ASCII double-quotes ( " ) rather than typographic curly quotes. On an editorial, trust-forward surface — a real customer's words shown to the operator before they approve a reply — dumb quotes read as unfinished and cheapen the moment. Curly quotes are essentially absent app-wide (one file only), so this isn't a localized slip; the app has no typographic-punctuation discipline.
issues.evidence: src/components/cockpit/approval-card.tsx:178,180 (>"); curly-quote grep across src → 1 file total

issues.title: --ink-1 is undefined in the Inbox confirm-sheet money value
issues.detail: swipe-decision-card.module.css is the Home/editorial register where only --ink, --ink-2/3/4 are defined (the --ink-1 token lives in inbox-design-base.css, scoped to .inbox-page/.sheet, a different subtree). Line 446 sets .confirmRowValue { color: var(--ink-1) } with no fallback, so the financial amount the operator reads right before confirming a spend resolves to an undefined custom property and falls back to inherited color. The single most consequential number in the flow is styled through a typo'd token.
issues.evidence: src/components/decisions/swipe-decision-card.module.css:446 (color: var(--ink-1)); inbox-design-base.css:22 defines --ink-1 only under .inbox-page/.sheet

issues.title: Two numeric APIs (tabular-nums vs tnum) used interchangeably with no rule
issues.detail: Mercury/Results use font-variant-numeric:tabular-nums; Home/Inbox/agent-panel use font-feature-settings:'tnum'. These aren't equivalent: font-feature-settings is the low-level escape hatch and OVERRIDES rather than composes, so any future feature (e.g. slashed-zero ss01, used on the landing page) silently won't stack on a tnum element. It's a latent footgun and a sign the numeric treatment was never centralized into one figure utility.
issues.evidence: globals.css:627 vs this-week.module.css:87/inbox.css:108; landing uses fontFeatureSettings '"tnum","ss01"' (pricing.tsx:115)

issues.title: Uppercase-label letter-spacing is unsystematized (0.04em → 0.18em, ~9 values)
issues.detail: Mono/uppercase labels — the connective tissue of the editorial look (folios, eyebrows, KPI labels, section labels) — are tracked at 0.04, 0.05, 0.06, 0.07, 0.08, 0.10, 0.12, 0.14, 0.16, 0.18em with no scale. .section-label is 0.07em, Home eyebrows 0.10em, cockpit labels 0.12–0.14em, folios 0.12em. The differences are visible at small sizes and make the small-caps system feel hand-tuned per component rather than designed once.
issues.evidence: globals.css:403 (.section-label 0.07em); home.module.css:65 (0.1em); kpi-strip.tsx:37 / status-pill.tsx:32 (0.14em); folio 0.12em

issues.title: --font-display declares a font that is never loaded
issues.detail: globals.css:85 sets --font-display: 'Instrument Sans', but layout.tsx binds DM Sans (not Instrument Sans) to the --font-display next/font variable, and Instrument Sans is imported nowhere. The :root declaration is dead/misleading — login, reset-password, and settings/identity headlines (28 consumers) actually render DM Sans while the CSS claims Instrument Sans. A maintainer reading the token will mis-reason about the display face.
issues.evidence: src/app/globals.css:85 vs src/app/layout.tsx:21-26; grep 'Instrument' → only the dead globals.css line

issues.title: Space Mono is loaded but split-brain with JetBrains Mono and has no documented rule
issues.detail: Two monospace faces ship: Space Mono (--font-mono, Tailwind font-mono utility) renders technical strings — serviceIds, error refs, phone numbers in Settings (~15 sites) — while JetBrains Mono (--font-mono-editorial / var(--mono)) renders editorial mono — folios, timestamps, KPI labels. They're visibly different typefaces doing the same 'monospace' job in adjacent surfaces, with nothing documenting the split. Either consolidate or name the distinction (e.g. --font-mono-system vs --font-mono-editorial) explicitly.
issues.evidence: layout.tsx:28-33 (Space_Mono → --font-mono); settings/connections-list.tsx:181, whatsapp-management.tsx:236 (font-mono); vs globals.css:186 var(--mono)=JetBrains

opportunities.title: Give the cockpit a real type scale and adopt the editorial serif for agent identity
opportunities.detail: Extend tokens.ts with a typography block (or better, retire the inline-px cockpit type and consume the same --serif/--mono/--font-home-sans + a shared size scale used by Home/Inbox). At minimum: render the agent name in identity.tsx and the approval title in approval-card.tsx using var(--serif) italic (matching .fromName/.title in swipe-decision-card) so Alex's voice is continuous from Inbox into the cockpit. This is the highest-impact single change for product cohesion.

opportunities.title: Ship one canonical numeric-figure utility and a single 'metric number' type style
opportunities.detail: Define .num / [data-tabular] once in globals.css with font-variant-numeric:tabular-nums (+ optional slashed-zero) and a chosen metric typeface, then apply it to every KPI/ROI/hero number. Pick ONE face for big numbers (the JetBrains-mono treatment Results already uses reads most 'instrument-grade' for a revenue OS) and make Home/cockpit match. Fixes the jitter, the three-fonts problem, and the tnum/tabular-nums split in one move.

opportunities.title: Add a typographic-punctuation pass: curly quotes, real primes, hair-spaces
opportunities.detail: Replace ASCII " with curly quotes in pull-quotes (approval-card), ensure all em-dashes/ellipses are real glyphs, and consider a tiny smart-quote helper for agent-authored copy (testimonials, weeknotes, verdict lines). On a 'words from your customer / your agent' product, punctuation polish is disproportionately felt as trust and care.

opportunities.title: Systematize label tracking into 2–3 named steps
opportunities.detail: Collapse the 9 ad-hoc letter-spacing values for uppercase/mono labels into a small scale (e.g. --track-label 0.08em for ~10–11px caps, --track-eyebrow 0.12em for tiny mono eyebrows) and reference it everywhere. The small-caps system is doing real editorial work; making it consistent makes it feel authored once, not per-component.

opportunities.title: Delete the dead Instrument Sans fallback and document the two-mono rule
opportunities.detail: Either load Instrument Sans or change --font-display's fallback to DM Sans so the token tells the truth; and add a one-line comment/decision: Space Mono = system/technical strings, JetBrains Mono = editorial. Cheap correctness wins that prevent future maintainers from reasoning against a fictional font (this codebase already has a memory note about exactly this class of 'token lies' bug).

topMoves: Unify the agent voice: make the cockpit (identity.tsx, approval-card.tsx, KPI strip/tile) consume the editorial serif for agent names + titles and a shared type scale instead of inline-px Inter, so Alex/Riley/Mira read the same from Inbox to cockpit.

Ship ONE metric-number treatment: a single .num/[data-tabular] utility with real tabular figures and one chosen typeface, applied to every KPI/ROI/hero number — this kills the data-tabular no-op jitter, the serif-vs-mono-vs-Inter three-fonts split, and the tnum/tabular-nums inconsistency at once.

Fix the trust-critical money typography: replace var(--ink-1) in swipe-decision-card .confirmRowValue (line 446) with a defined token so the spend amount before confirm isn't styled through an undefined property.
Run a typographic-punctuation pass — curly quotes on the approval pull-quote (approval-card.tsx:178/180) and app-wide — so customer/agent words feel finished.

Systematize the small-caps label system: collapse the ~9 ad-hoc letter-spacing values into 2–3 named tracking tokens, and delete/correct the dead 'Instrument Sans' --font-display fallback so tokens stop lying about which fonts render.

surface: Color, contrast & accessibility (whole app — globals.css tokens + Home, Inbox, Results, cockpit)
feelVerdict: Visually this is the calmest, most editorial slice in the category — the warm-cream/ink/amber palette genuinely feels premium and trustworthy, and the team clearly cares (reduced-motion is handled, dialogs have aria-modal, several focus rings exist). But under the surface the accessibility floor is soft in exactly the places that carry the most trust: the ONE amber "approve / spend my money" button fails WCAG AA contrast at rest (3.76:1), the entire Inbox decision card is a mouse/touch-only div with no keyboard path, and there is no global focus-visible style so most controls have no visible focus. It looks accessible and calm; it is not yet reliably operable or legible for a keyboard or low-vision medspa owner.

strengths.title: Reduced-motion is respected globally and per-component
strengths.detail: A global prefers-reduced-motion block neutralizes animation/transition/scroll, and the onboarding tiers add a second guard. This is best-in-class and rare — most teams skip it entirely.

strengths.title: Detail sheets are real dialogs, not just visual overlays
strengths.detail: Approval and handoff sheets carry role=dialog + aria-modal=true + an aria-labelled close button, and Esc closes them. This is the correct modal contract and the keyboard story inside the sheet is sound.
strengths.evidence: approval-detail-sheet.tsx:145-149 + handoff-detail-sheet.tsx:54-58; Esc handler in inbox-screen.tsx:153-160

strengths.title: Color is not the SOLE signal in the best places
strengths.detail: Status pills pair color with an uppercase text LABEL (WORKING/WAITING/HALTED), and Results deltas embed a directional glyph (↑/↓/—) in the wire text rather than relying on hue. This shows real color-only-signaling awareness.
strengths.evidence: status-pill.tsx:24-38 (label + Dot); delta-badge.tsx:6-16 (`wire text already carries the glyph`)

strengths.title: Editorial 'ink' text ramp on cream is mostly legible
strengths.detail: The primary/secondary text tiers clear AA on the warm-cream body: muted-foreground 5.47:1, ink-2 and the deep agent tones (coral-deep 4.56:1, teal-deep 6.69:1, violet-deep 6.91:1) all pass for body text. The core reading experience is solid.
strengths.evidence: Computed from globals.css --muted-foreground 30 6% 40% and inbox-design-base.css --coral-deep/#1f6663/#5f4f95 on #fff

strengths.title: Identity-vs-action color discipline is explicit and enforced
strengths.detail: Agent hues (coral/teal/violet) are confined to avatars, name text, and 3px stripes; amber is the only action color. This is stated as a locked rule in multiple files and actually followed — a genuinely premium, non-arbitrary system.
strengths.evidence: home.module.css:7-8 + :448-451 (`identity color may NEVER back an action button`); inbox.css:171 (`agent identity stripe — orientation, not action`)

issues.title: The ONE amber primary button fails WCAG AA contrast at rest (white-on-amber = 3.76:1)
issues.detail: hsl(30 55% 46%) / #c97b1a with #fff text at 14px/600 computes to 3.76:1 — below the 4.5:1 AA floor for normal text (600-weight 14px does NOT qualify as 'large'). This is the single most important control in the product: the button a medspa owner taps to approve spending money and to send messages to their customers. The failing state is the RESTING state; only :hover (40% L) reaches 4.58:1, so the default a user reads fails. The same amber+white appears on every primary CTA across Home, the Inbox detail sheet, the swipe-approve reveal zone, and the cockpit.
issues.evidence: globals.css:52 `--operator:30 55% 46%`; inbox.css:907-910 `.ds-action-primary{background:var(--amber);color:#fff}` at :895-896 14px/600; home.module.css:474-476 `.btnPrimary`

issues.title: The entire Inbox decision card is keyboard-inaccessible (non-interactive div, no tabindex/role/key handler)
issues.detail: InboxDecisionCard renders the whole card body as `<div className="decision-track">` wired only to onClick/onMouseDown/onMouseMove/onTouchStart. There is no tabIndex, no role=button, and no onKeyDown. The card is the PRIMARY unit of the Inbox and the only doorway to the detail sheet where approve/decline live. A keyboard or screen-reader user cannot focus it, cannot open detail, and cannot act. The one focusable child is the avatar button — which opens the agent panel, NOT the decision. Approvals are also swipe-right or tap only, with zero keyboard equivalent on the doorway. This is a hard WCAG 2.1.1 (Keyboard) failure on the core flow.
issues.evidence: inbox-decision-card.tsx:102-117 (div.decision-track with pointer-only handlers, no tabIndex/role/onKeyDown); grep for tabIndex/role=button/onKeyDown in components/inbox returns nothing

issues.title: No global focus-visible style — most interactive controls have no visible keyboard focus
issues.detail: globals.css defines a --ring token but ZERO :focus/:focus-visible rules. Focus rings are added ad hoc on only ~4 elements (home agent chip, this-week foot link, results agent button, inbox avatar). Everything else has none: the primary/secondary/dismiss buttons in the Inbox detail sheet (.ds-action only styles :disabled/:hover/:active), the filter chips, the swipe-track card, the scrim, and every inline-styled cockpit button in approval-card.tsx. Worse, the sheet textareas explicitly do `outline:none` and signal focus ONLY by a border-color shift to --ink-1 (a non-text-affecting cue that can fail 1.4.11). For a keyboard user the approve/decline buttons light up on hover but show nothing on Tab.
issues.evidence: No focus rules in globals.css (grep empty); inbox.css:907-936 `.ds-action-primary/secondary/dismiss` have no :focus-visible; inbox.css:968 + :1276 + :1306 `outline:none` with only `:focus{border-color:var(--ink-1)}`

issues.title: Tertiary text and the ink-4 tier fail contrast and are used for real, scannable information
issues.detail: --tertiary-foreground (30 5% 58%) on warm-white = 2.90:1 (FAIL); it backs .section-label small-caps. --ink-4 (≈20 6% 62%) = 2.39:1 on cream / 2.70:1 on white and the inbox --ink-4 (#bfb7ac) = 1.98:1 on white — well below even the 3:1 large-text floor. ink-4 is not decorative: it colors Results metric LABELS (heroRevenueLabel, worthItLabel, heroStatLabel), the empty-state polling meta, and the cockpit ink4 (#A39786 = 2.70:1) used for quote attributions and ROI sub-labels. Time-poor owners glancing at ROI labels and 'cost per lead' captions are reading near-invisible text.
issues.evidence: globals.css:23 --tertiary-foreground; globals.css:178 --ink-4; results.module.css:78/121/150/304 `color:var(--ink-4)`; inbox-design-base.css:25 `--ink-4:#bfb7ac`; tokens.ts:7 `ink4:"#A39786"`

issues.title: Results buries bad news: a metric moving the WRONG way looks identical to a flat one
issues.detail: By the 'no red/green' rule, .deltaPos (good) is amber but .deltaNeg (revenue DOWN / a worsening metric) is var(--ink-3) and .deltaFlat is var(--ink-4) — two muted grays a half-step apart. On an ROI screen the entire emotional difference between 'your money is working' and 'your money is leaking' is carried by a small ↑/↓ glyph in near-identical low-contrast gray (ink-3 ≈3.6:1, ink-4 ≈2.4:1). It is not a strict color-only failure (the glyph exists), but it is a trust/clarity failure: declines are visually suppressed exactly where an operator most needs to notice them, and the down-arrow is the lowest-contrast element on the page.
issues.evidence: results.module.css:18-30 (`.deltaPos{color:hsl(var(--action))}` vs `.deltaNeg{color:var(--ink-3)}` vs `.deltaFlat{color:var(--ink-4)}`); comment :4 `NO red/green. amber is the ONE positive signal`

issues.title: Three parallel color-token systems with colliding names — high drift/regression surface
issues.detail: The same concepts are defined three incompatible ways: (1) globals.css HSL-triplet custom props (hsl(var(--x))), (2) inbox-design-base.css literal-hex tokens (--coral/--ink-1/--amber/--good) scoped to .inbox-page/.sheet, and (3) cockpit tokens.ts hardcoded-hex `T` object. The agent-amber alone has at least three values: hsl(30 55% 46%)=#c97b1a (app/inbox), #B8782E (cockpit T.amber), #A07850 (--sw-accent). The inbox file's own header documents that a naming collision already silently dropped backgrounds to transparent once. This guarantees that any contrast/a11y fix made in one system will NOT propagate to the other two, and that 'fix the amber button' must be done 3+ times or it regresses.
issues.evidence: inbox-design-base.css:1-13 (collision warning) + :41 `--amber:#c97b1a`; tokens.ts:11 `amber:"#B8782E"`; globals.css:96 `--sw-accent:#A07850` vs :52 `--operator:30 55% 46%`

issues.title: Loading state is an unannounced, low-effort bare-text div
issues.detail:
The Inbox loading branch renders `
Loading…
` with no role=status / aria-live, so screen-reader users get no announcement that content is pending, and it visually breaks the otherwise-editorial surface (plain text, no skeleton matching the card rhythm the rest of Home uses). First-load on a slow medspa connection — the most common first impression — is the least considered state.
issues.evidence:
inbox-screen.tsx:181-183 `if(filtered.isLoading) return
Loading…
` (no aria-live; no .inbox-loading skeleton styling found in inbox.css)

opportunities.title: Darken the action amber to a single AA-passing token and alias all three systems to it
opportunities.detail: Move --operator/--action from 46% L to ≈40% L (hsl(30 55% 40%) ≈ 4.58:1 white-on-amber, passing AA for normal text) and make the cockpit T.amber and inbox --amber literal-resolve to the SAME value. Keep the brighter 46% only for non-text uses (stripes, progress fills, the brand pip). One change fixes the most-used control everywhere and removes the 'fix it 3x' tax.

opportunities.title: Make the decision card a real button and add a global focus-visible ring
opportunities.detail: Wrap the decision-track in a focusable role=button (tabIndex=0 + onKeyDown Enter/Space → onOpenDetail) so keyboard users reach the detail sheet where approve/decline already work; keep swipe as a pointer enhancement. Add ONE globals.css rule — `:focus-visible{outline:2px solid hsl(var(--ring));outline-offset:2px}` — so every control (incl. .ds-action buttons and the inline cockpit buttons) gets a visible ring by default instead of the current opt-in patchwork.

opportunities.title: Promote the failing micro-text tiers up one step
opportunities.detail: Retire --tertiary-foreground (58% L) and --ink-4-as-text usages: bump section labels to muted-foreground (5.47:1) and Results metric labels to ink-3-or-darker. Reserve ink-4 strictly for hairlines/decoration. Labels like 'cost per lead' and 'ad spend' on Results are load-bearing for trust and should clear 4.5:1.

opportunities.title: Let decline/regression read as gently 'cool', not invisible
opportunities.detail: Without reintroducing alarmist red, give .deltaNeg a slightly cooler, slightly higher-contrast treatment (e.g. a muted slate distinct from flat-gray, or a subtle weight bump on the down-glyph) so a worsening ROI metric is glanceable. Keeping amber=good but making bad-news legible is the trust move; suppressing it reads as hiding the ball.

opportunities.title: Give Inbox a skeleton loading state with an aria-live announcement
opportunities.detail: Replace 'Loading…' with 2-3 card-shaped shimmer rows that match the queue rhythm, wrapped in role=status/aria-busy, so the first-load impression is calm and continuous and SR users hear that content is on the way.

topMoves: Darken the action amber from 46%→~40% lightness (white-on-amber 3.76:1→4.58:1, AA) and resolve cockpit T.amber + inbox --amber to that same token, so the primary 'approve/spend' button passes contrast everywhere at once.
Make the Inbox decision card keyboard-operable: role=button + tabIndex=0 + Enter/Space→onOpenDetail, since today the core approve flow is mouse/touch-only (WCAG 2.1.1 failure).

Add a single global :focus-visible outline rule in globals.css so the detail-sheet approve/decline buttons, filter chips, swipe card, and inline cockpit buttons all get a visible keyboard focus ring instead of today's ~4-element opt-in.
Promote the failing low-contrast text tiers (--tertiary-foreground 2.90:1, --ink-4 ~2.4-2.7:1) up one step wherever they carry real info — Results metric labels, section labels, cockpit captions.
Stop burying bad news on Results: give a worsening delta a distinct, higher-contrast cool treatment (not red, not the same muted gray as 'flat') so a declining ROI is glanceable.

feelVerdict: The motion vocabulary is genuinely premium — named easing tokens, a calm character with real GPU discipline, and a beautifully choreographed confirm sheet — but it's applied unevenly and stops short exactly where operators feel it most. The hero swipe-to-approve gesture is excellent on the way in and then snaps awkwardly on the way out (a dead exit-animation hook, no list collapse, a reduced-motion teleport), the daily Home briefing hard-cuts with no entrance, and the surfaces people use every day fall back to a bare "Loading…" while the marketing page gets the polished skeleton. It feels like a designer's intent that the engineering only finished about 70% of the way.

strengths.title: Motion tokens are a designed system, not ad-hoc magic numbers
strengths.detail: globals.css defines a coherent scale: --ease-standard/enter/exit (Material curves), a fast/default/slow/very-slow duration ramp, semantic onboarding tokens (--transition-structural/feedback/trust), and a distinct --ease-home spring reserved for the warm editorial surfaces. A named 'trust' easing at 600ms is a thoughtful touch for a trust-centric app.

strengths.title: The character is animated with genuine performance discipline
strengths.detail: OperatorCharacter avoids filter:blur for the aura (which forces a per-frame GPU layer) in favor of a soft radial gradient, pauses animation via animationPlayState when the tab is hidden, and re-pulses the aura on config change via a keyed remount. The float + breathe loops are slow (6s) and calm, not fidgety.

strengths.title: Reduced-motion degrades meaningfully, not just by stopping
strengths.detail: Beyond the global reset, the important loops degrade with intent: the armed Approve button swaps its pulse for a static 3px focus ring so the 'primed' affordance survives, and the reports shimmer goes static-but-visible. A more honest reduced-motion story than most apps ship.
strengths.evidence: src/app/globals.css:308-315 (global reset); src/components/decisions/swipe-decision-card.module.css:281-289 (armed to static ring fallback)

strengths.title: The confirm sheet has a properly choreographed, platform-aware entrance
strengths.detail: The scrim fades (240ms) while the sheet slides up from translateY(100%) on mobile, then switches to a centered scale(0.96) to 1 + fade dialog at >=768px — two correct idioms for two form factors, both on --ease-home, with the mobile drag-handle hidden on desktop. This is the bar the rest of the app should meet.

strengths.title: The swipe gesture physics themselves feel hand-tuned and intentional
strengths.detail: use-card-swipe.ts implements axis-lock with a 6px deadzone (so vertical scroll isn't hijacked), a rubber-band with a 0.65 power-curve resist for blocked right-swipes, undamped intent tracking separate from the damped visual, and trailing-click suppression so a drag isn't misread as a tap. This detail separates a real swipe from a toy one.

issues.title: The Inbox card's exit animation is wired to a CSS rule that does not exist — committed cards have no choreographed departure
issues.detail: InboxDecisionCard sets data-exiting=true on commit, but inbox-decision-card.css is a 27-line stub with only avatar-button styles — there is no [data-exiting] selector anywhere in the inbox CSS. The .decision-track has transition: transform 320ms so a committed card slides 600px sideways, but the row it leaves collapses instantly (no height/opacity animation) and the data-exiting hook is dead. Worse, useCardSwipe fires the commit callback after EXIT_MS=280ms while the CSS transition is 320ms, so the mutation/refetch starts before the slide visually finishes — the slide and the list mutation race. Cards lurch out and the queue snaps closed: the antithesis of calm.
issues.evidence: src/components/inbox/inbox-decision-card.tsx:85 (data-exiting set) vs src/components/inbox/inbox-decision-card.css (27 lines, no exit rule); EXIT_MS=280 at use-card-swipe.ts:10 vs track transition 320ms at inbox.css:196

issues.title: No optimistic UI on approve/skip — the card sits frozen for a full network round-trip before anything happens
issues.detail: useRecommendationAction only invalidateQueries onSuccess; there is no onMutate optimistic removal (the ONLY onMutate in the app is the unrelated contacts stage-transition hook). So on the primary action of the entire product — approving a decision — the operator taps/swipes, the card freezes in place, and it only disappears once the POST returns and the feed refetches. On mobile/flaky connections that's a visible dead beat where the user wonders if the tap registered. Best-in-class apps remove the item instantly and reconcile in the background.
issues.evidence: src/hooks/use-recommendation-action.ts:36-42 (invalidate-only, no onMutate); only onMutate in src is src/app/(auth)/(mercury)/contacts/hooks/use-opportunity-stage-transition.ts:50

issues.title: The three core authed surfaces (Home, Inbox, Results) have no loading.tsx — first paint is blank or a bare Loading… string
issues.detail: Only (public)/loading.tsx exists, and it's a polished multi-block skeleton — for the MARKETING page. The authed app has no route-level skeletons (no (auth)/loading.tsx, /inbox/loading.tsx, /results/loading.tsx), and the Inbox's in-component loading state is literally a div with the text 'Loading…' — unstyled, no skeleton. The perceived-performance investment is backwards: the page seen once gets the skeleton; the pages hit 20x/day get a blank flash or a 1995-era Loading…
issues.evidence: src/components/inbox/inbox-screen.tsx:181-183 (plain Loading… text); only src/app/(public)/loading.tsx exists

issues.title: Under reduced-motion, a committed swipe card teleports 600px instead of calmly cross-fading
issues.detail: Commit sets position via inline style transform translateX(dx) with dx=600. The global reduced-motion reset only zeroes transition-duration/animation — it cannot neutralize an inline transform value, and inbox.css has no @media (prefers-reduced-motion) block at all. So a reduced-motion (often vestibular-sensitive) user who approves sees the card jump 600px across the screen with zero easing, then the row vanishes — arguably more jarring than the motion they opted out of. The correct reduced-motion remove is an opacity fade or instant removal, never a full-width positional jump.
issues.evidence: src/components/decisions/use-card-swipe.ts:120-121 (setDx(600)); inbox.css has zero prefers-reduced-motion matches

issues.title: framer-motion@12 is a paid dependency that is never used — so layout/list transitions are simply absent
issues.detail: package.json ships framer-motion ^12.38.0, but there is not a single AnimatePresence, motion., or layout import in src/. The app pays the bundle cost of a best-in-class animation library while leaving the exact problems it solves — enter/exit choreography, automatic FLIP layout animation on list removal, shared-element transitions — entirely unsolved. This is precisely why card removal snaps: nothing wraps the decisions.map() to animate the height collapse.
issues.evidence: package.json:42 (framer-motion ^12.38.0); zero AnimatePresence/motion. usages in src; raw .map at src/components/inbox/inbox-screen.tsx:207-214

issues.title: Home has zero entrance choreography despite the infrastructure being built for it
issues.detail: A useEntrancePlayed hook (sessionStorage play-once-per-session) and a FadeIn/useScrollReveal system exist — but they're used only by the v6 marketing landing. HomePage (317 lines) imports none of them. The daily verdict briefing — the emotional centerpiece a returning operator sees first each morning — hard-cuts into existence with no settle, no stagger down the bento, no sense of the agents reporting in. The fade-in keyframe and once-per-session gate exist precisely for this and aren't wired.
issues.evidence: src/hooks/use-entrance-played.ts (consumed only by landing); src/components/home/home-page.tsx (no FadeIn/entrance/stagger); fade-in keyframe globals.css:441-444 unused on Home

issues.title: No global route-transition or in-flight feedback — navigation is a hard cut into a pending void
issues.detail: Nav links use only transition-colors duration-fast (a hover tint). With no (auth) loading.tsx and no top progress-bar/NProgress indicator, tapping Inbox to Results to Home is an abrupt content swap with a possible blank beat while the route segment streams. The tap registers (color change) but the consequence is silent until data lands.
issues.evidence: src/components/layout/app-sidebar.tsx:83 (transition-colors only); no loading.tsx under (auth); no progress-bar component found

opportunities.title: Wrap the decision queues in framer-motion AnimatePresence with layout animation
opportunities.detail: Since framer-motion is already installed, wrap decisions.map() (Inbox) and the NeedsYou visible list (Home) in AnimatePresence with motion.div layout, an exit that slides + collapses height, and a subtle staggered enter. A removed card glides out AND the cards below smoothly close the gap (FLIP) instead of snapping. It also retires the dead data-exiting attribute and the EXIT_MS-vs-320ms race by letting the library own exit timing. One change fixes the snap on both surfaces and finally justifies the dependency.

opportunities.title: Add optimistic removal to useRecommendationAction (onMutate)
opportunities.detail: On approve/skip, optimistically remove the decision from the cached feed in onMutate, reconcile on settle, and roll back on error (surfacing the existing error/undo paths). Combined with AnimatePresence the card leaves the instant the operator acts — the Linear/Superhuman it-already-happened feel — instead of freezing for the round-trip.

opportunities.title: Ship real loading skeletons for Home, Inbox, and Results as route-level loading.tsx
opportunities.detail: Replace the bare Loading… with structural skeletons that mirror each surface (verdict hero + 2 decision-card placeholders + bento rail on Home; queue rows on Inbox; KPI tiles + funnel on Results), reusing the reports shimmer keyframe for consistency. The marketing page already demonstrates the quality bar — bring it to the surfaces operators use daily.

opportunities.title: Give Home a once-per-session staggered entrance
opportunities.detail: Wire the already-built useEntrancePlayed gate + fade-in keyframe into HomePage: on the first load of a session the Verdict rises and settles first (~380ms), then NeedsYou/TeamPulse/ThisWeek stagger in at ~60-80ms intervals; subsequent same-session visits render instantly. This is the team-reporting-in feeling, at near-zero risk because the infra and reduced-motion handling already exist.

opportunities.title: Add an explicit reduced-motion path for card removal plus a thin global top-loader
opportunities.detail: In useCardSwipe, branch on matchMedia reduced-motion at commit: fade opacity to 0 (or remove immediately) instead of setDx(600), and add an @media(prefers-reduced-motion) block to inbox.css for parity. Separately, a 2px operator-amber top progress bar that activates on route navigation and while a recommendation mutation is pending gives every tap a continuous I-heard-you pulse without per-surface work.

topMoves: Make card removal feel intentional: wrap the Inbox + Home decision lists in framer-motion AnimatePresence (layout/FLIP) so a committed card glides out and the queue smoothly closes the gap — killing the dead data-exiting hook and the 280ms-vs-320ms race.

Add optimistic onMutate removal to useRecommendationAction so approve/skip removes the card instantly and reconciles in the background, eliminating the frozen-card network dead-beat on the product's primary action.

Ship route-level loading.tsx skeletons for Home, Inbox, and Results (reusing the reports shimmer) and delete the bare Loading… string — give the daily surfaces the same polish the marketing page already has.

Wire the existing useEntrancePlayed gate + fade-in keyframe into HomePage for a calm once-per-session staggered settle of the verdict and bento — the agents-reporting-in moment that's currently a hard cut.

Fix the reduced-motion teleport: at swipe commit, cross-fade or instant-remove instead of translateX(600px), and add a prefers-reduced-motion block to inbox.css; optionally add a thin amber top-loader for route + mutation in-flight feedback.

surface: Information architecture & navigation (whole app) — (auth)/layout.tsx, components/layout/* shell + nav + drawers, and the route tree (Home, /inbox, /results, /alex /riley /mira, /settings/*, legacy (mercury): /activity /automations /contacts /reports)
feelVerdict: Calm and editorially tasteful at a glance, but the underlying IA is quietly incoherent: the nav model changes shape three times across breakpoints, the same nouns ("Inbox", "Reports") point at two different surfaces, and the product's core concept — agents — has no nav home and behaves differently depending on where you tap it. It reads as several good redesigns layered without a reconciliation pass, so a busy operator gets a pretty surface they can't quite build one mental model of.

strengths.title: Genuinely restrained, glanceable primary nav
strengths.detail: Top-level IA is just Home / Inbox / Results — three nouns, no menus, no jargon. For a time-poor non-technical operator this is the right altitude and the courage to say no to a kitchen-sink dashboard.

strengths.title: Disciplined chrome-free / gate logic with documented intent
strengths.detail: The shell carefully strips chrome for /onboarding and /operator and gates onboarding-incomplete users, using a canonical prefix-match shape with comments explaining each exemption — careful IA plumbing, not accidental.

strengths.title: One shared editorial shell mounted exactly once
strengths.detail: All authed routes inherit a single header + providers (Halt, RightDrawer, AmbientCream), so nav chrome is consistent and halt state is app-global by construction — the right backbone.

strengths.title: Forensic/advanced surfaces are deliberately hidden, not deleted
strengths.detail: /activity (audit ledger) and full /reports are kept reachable by URL but intentionally absent from the operator Tools menu, matching the 'clinic owners want bookings, not an event log' thesis.

issues.title: The primary nav is three different navigations across breakpoints, and they don't agree on what exists
issues.detail: Below 768px the primary nav is a fixed bottom bar of Home/Inbox/Results. From 768–1023px that same nav becomes an inline horizontal strip in the header. At ≥1024px it is hidden entirely (hide-at-lg) and replaced by a left sidebar exposing a DIFFERENT, larger set — Pipeline, Automations, Mira, Full reports, Settings — none of which exist in the mobile or tablet nav. So 'what can I navigate to?' depends on window width, and the richer set is desktop-only. A phone-first medspa owner never sees Pipeline/Automations/Mira in nav; they're buried in a header 'Tools ▾' dropdown. There is no single stable map of the app.
issues.evidence: globals.css:516 (.app-sidebar{display:none}) + :538 (.hide-at-lg{display:none} at ≥1024); app-sidebar.tsx:33-65 (sidebar adds Pipeline/Automations/Mira/Full reports) vs primary-nav.tsx:5-9 (only 3 items)

issues.title: "Inbox" is two different destinations in the same chrome — a drawer AND a page — on the identical feed
issues.detail: The header renders an Inbox button opening a right-side Sheet (InboxDrawer) of decisions; primary-nav also has an 'Inbox' link to the full /inbox page (InboxScreen); the mobile bottom bar has 'Inbox' too. All three read the same useDecisionFeed. At 768–1023px 'Inbox' appears twice in the header (inline nav link + drawer trigger) pointing at two different surfaces. On the highest-stakes surface (approving spend / customer messages), an operator can't tell which is canonical or whether acting in one reflects in the other.
issues.evidence: inbox-drawer.tsx:47,101-120 (Sheet trigger 'Inbox', useDecisionFeed) vs inbox/page.tsx:1-5 → inbox-screen.tsx:8 (same useDecisionFeed); editorial-auth-shell.tsx:42,45-47 (InboxDrawer + inline PrimaryNav co-mounted); primary-nav.tsx:7

issues.title: Agents have no nav home, and 'opening an agent' does two contradictory things depending on location
issues.detail: Agents are the product's central metaphor, yet there is zero nav entry to reach one. /alex and /riley were retired and redirect to /?agent=alex which opens a read-only AgentPanel SHEET on Home. But Settings → Team renders agent cards that router.push into a full /settings/team/[id] ROUTE. So tapping 'Alex' on Home opens an overlay you dismiss back to Home, while tapping 'Alex' in Settings navigates into a sub-page with a back link — same entity, two interaction models, two locations, neither in the primary nav. Mira is yet a third model (real pages /mira, /mira/review, /mira/creatives/[id], reachable only via Tools when opt-in, plus its own panel). A new operator cannot form one rule for 'how do I check on an agent.'
issues.evidence: alex/page.tsx:6 + riley/page.tsx:6 (redirect /?agent=); home-page.tsx:239 (TeamPulse onOpenAgent=setPanelAgent → sheet) + :301-313 (AgentPanel sheet) vs settings/team/page.tsx:184,198 (router.push to /settings/team/[id]); mira/page.tsx:8-12 (notFound unless enabled)

issues.title: The help-overlay — the one place the app teaches its own IA — describes navigation that no longer exists
issues.detail: Pressing '?' opens 'Quick reference,' the canonical onboarding-into-the-model. It says 'Each agent has a home page with their own work.' That is false post-retirement: /alex and /riley redirect to a panel; only Mira has pages, only when enabled. A first-time operator adopts this wrong mental model and hunts for an agent 'home page' that isn't in any nav. Worse, this is the single source of IA truth and it actively misleads.
issues.evidence: help-overlay.tsx:76-79 ('Three agents work… Each agent has a home page with their own work') contradicted by alex/page.tsx:3-6, riley/page.tsx:3-6, app-shell.tsx:47-48 comment ('/alex and /riley were retired')

issues.title: 'Results', 'Reports', and 'Full reports' are three overlapping outcome labels, two of which resolve to the same route
issues.detail: Primary nav has 'Results' (→/results). The sidebar TOOLS array also defines 'Reports' → /results (deduped away), then appends 'Full reports' → /reports (legacy Mercury). The Tools dropdown shows 'Advanced → Full reports' → /reports too. So an operator sees up to three outcome nouns — Results, Reports, Full reports — two targeting the same page and one a parallel legacy surface for the same attribution/funnel/cost data, with no label cue explaining the difference. The legacy-vs-new-register seam leaks straight into nav vocabulary.
issues.evidence: app-sidebar.tsx:42 ({label:'Reports',href:'/results'}) + :58-59 ({label:'Full reports',href:'/reports'}); tools-overflow.tsx:25 ('Reports'→/results) + :93-97 ('Advanced'/'Full reports'→/reports); results/page.tsx vs (mercury)/reports/page.tsx

issues.title: Two halt controls sit adjacent in the header, and the keyboard model is effectively undiscoverable
issues.detail: The header mounts BOTH the LiveSignalPopover (its panel has a Halt/Resume action) AND a standalone HaltButtonClient ('Halt'/'Halted'), side by side. Two affordances for the single most consequential action (stop all agents spending money / messaging clients) invites 'which is real / did it work?' hesitation and they can momentarily disagree visually. Separately, the only place shortcuts (?, H to halt, Esc) are advertised is a footer line INSIDE the Live popover — you must open an obscure popover to learn that popover's own shortcuts. 'H halts everything' is a powerful, hidden gesture with no visible hint in the chrome.
issues.evidence: editorial-auth-shell.tsx:41-43 (LiveSignalPopover + HaltButtonClient together); live-signal-popover.tsx:108 (in-popover Halt/Resume) + :136-138 (shortcut hint only here); use-keyboard-shortcuts.ts:30-33 ('h' toggles halt, no visible hint)

issues.title: Settings IA is internally inconsistent: index lists 3 areas, the rail lists 7, and the index copy is Alex-specific for agent-agnostic sections
issues.detail: The /settings index ('Choose what you want to tune') shows 3 cards — Playbook, Team, Channels — while SettingsLayout's sidebar/mobile list exposes 7 (adds Knowledge, Identity, Billing, Account). On desktop the landing contradicts the rail beside it; a mobile user landing on the 3-card index may believe Knowledge/Identity don't exist. The index copy frames everything around Alex ('tighten Alex's playbook', 'Adjust your AI roster…operator handoff rules') even though Team/Channels govern all three agents. Playbook/Knowledge/Identity also overlap conceptually ('what the agents know/sound like') with no grouping to disambiguate.
issues.evidence: settings/page.tsx:3-19 (only Playbook/Team/Channels) + :28-29 (Alex-centric copy) vs settings-layout.tsx:10-18 (7 items incl. Knowledge/Identity/Billing/Account)

issues.title: Weak orientation in depth: no breadcrumbs, ad-hoc back paths, and active-state cues that fade at desktop
issues.detail: Deep routes (/settings/team/[agentId], /mira/creatives/[id]) and the AgentPanel/Inbox sheets have no breadcrumb; the only back path is a hand-rolled '← Settings' link that is mobile-only, plus sheet-dismiss. Active state is a 2px amber underline on mobile that DISAPPEARS at ≥768px (::after display:none), replaced by a faint 5% background tint — very low-contrast 'you are here.' Combined with the shifting nav model, an operator several levels deep on a phone has thin orientation and inconsistent escape routes.
issues.evidence: settings-layout.tsx:83-87 (mobile-only '← Settings', md:hidden); globals.css:1266-1268 (active underline width:100%) vs :1276-1281 (≥768 ::after display:none, active=bg 5%); no breadcrumb component in components/layout

opportunities.title: Collapse Inbox to one canonical surface and demote the other to a peek
opportunities.detail: Decide: either the drawer is a quick 'peek + act' that always ends with a 'View all in Inbox' footer to /inbox, OR /inbox is canonical and the header is just a count badge that routes there (no second Sheet of the same feed). Never label two adjacent things 'Inbox.' Killing the duplicate removes the 'did my approval register in both?' anxiety on the money surface.

opportunities.title: Make 'agents' a first-class, consistently-behaving nav concept
opportunities.detail: Add an 'Agents'/'Your team' destination listing Alex/Riley/Mira, and make tapping an agent do the SAME thing everywhere — pick the panel-sheet OR a route, not both. Reconcile Home's TeamPulse drill-in with Settings→Team→[id] into one 'agent detail,' then correct the help copy. The product's core metaphor should be the most navigable thing in it, not the least.

opportunities.title: Define ONE responsive nav model instead of three breakpoint personalities
opportunities.detail: Make the same set of destinations reachable at every width — e.g. promote the desktop sidebar set into the mobile/tablet model (a 'More' sheet rather than a header-only 'Tools ▾'), or accept a small fixed set everywhere and route the rest through a single consistent overflow. The available map of the app should not change with viewport.

opportunities.title: Resolve the Results/Reports legacy seam in nav vocabulary
opportunities.detail: One outcomes label. If /reports (Mercury) must persist for power users, surface it from INSIDE /results as a 'Detailed report' link, not as a third top-level noun ('Full reports') competing with 'Results' and 'Reports.' Delete the dead 'Reports→/results' TOOLS entry so the duplicate label never renders.

opportunities.title: Unify halt to one control and surface the keyboard model
opportunities.detail: Keep ONE halt affordance in the header (fold the standalone button into the Live popover, or vice versa). Add a discoverable shortcut entry point (a '?' icon or 'Shortcuts' in the account menu) so H/?/Esc aren't trapped inside the popover that documents them. For an audience trusting AI with money, the stop button must be singular and unmistakable.

opportunities.title: Make the Settings index mirror its sections and de-Alex the copy
opportunities.detail: List all 7 settings areas on the index (or drop the index and let the rail be the landing), and reword agent-agnostic sections away from 'Alex' (Team/Channels are workspace-level). Optionally group Playbook/Knowledge/Identity under 'What your agents know & sound like' so their overlap is explained rather than confusing.

topMoves: Collapse the dual Inbox: one canonical Inbox surface, the other becomes an explicit 'peek → View all' that links to it — never two header things labelled 'Inbox' on the same feed.

Give agents a real nav home and make 'open agent' behave identically everywhere (one of: panel-sheet OR route), then reconcile Home TeamPulse with Settings→Team→[id] and fix the help copy that promises non-existent agent home pages.

Adopt a single responsive nav model so the same destinations exist at every breakpoint — eliminate the mobile-bottom-bar vs tablet-inline vs desktop-sidebar three-way split where the desktop-only set (Pipeline/Automations/Mira) is otherwise unreachable from nav.
Collapse Results/Reports/'Full reports' into one outcomes label, nesting legacy /reports inside /results and deleting the duplicate 'Reports→/results' Tools entry.
Unify the two header halt controls into one unmistakable stop, and expose H/?/Esc shortcuts somewhere other than inside the popover that documents them.

surface: Interaction design: decision / approval / escalation flows (Inbox + Home decision cards, swipe-to-approve, approval & handoff detail sheets, confirm/undo, error recovery)
feelVerdict: The editorial chrome is genuinely beautiful — serif ledes, hairline risk pills, agent-identity stripes, a thoughtful rubber-band swipe with a "prime, don't commit" safety gate. But underneath the surface, the moment of truth (committing a real, money-spending or customer-facing action) feels unfinished and quietly anxious: the Undo toast is a generic un-themed shadcn rectangle that never auto-dismisses, the approval detail ships a literal "preview not yet wired / —" placeholder grid in production, there's zero in-flight feedback on the card while the mutation runs, and the SAME approve action has two different interaction models depending on whether you're on Home vs Inbox. It looks like a $100/mo premium colleague and behaves, at the decisive instant, like a prototype.

strengths.title: The risk-gated swipe is a genuinely smart, trustworthy safety model
strengths.detail: Swipe-right NEVER blindly commits. canSwipeApprove() only returns true for a present contract that is low-risk AND has no external/financial/client-facing effect; anything riskier rubber-bands back, primes the button with a pulse, and routes to detail. A missing contract is treated as unsafe. This is exactly the 'reversible by default, friction proportional to stakes' instinct the best products have — and it's enforced at the predicate, not inferred from copy.
strengths.evidence: src/lib/decisions/swipe-policy.ts:8 canSwipeApprove; use-card-swipe.ts:202-208 (swipe-right primes instead of commits); inbox-decision-card.tsx:57

strengths.title: The swipe physics are tuned with real craft
strengths.detail: Axis-lock dead-zone (6px) lets the list scroll vertically without hijack; the blocked right-swipe damps with a 0.65-power rubber-band capped at 110px so it feels alive but refuses to commit; intent is tracked on the UNDAMPED delta so the visual ceiling never gates the decision; a trailing synthetic-click suppressor stops a drag from being misread as a tap. This is the kind of detail Linear/Things ship.

strengths.title: Handoff detail sheet handles the hard delivery-failure case honestly
strengths.detail: The reply path distinguishes three outcomes — 200 delivered (close + 'handed back'), 502 saved-but-undelivered (keep sheet open, 'Saved — but we couldn't deliver it… reach out directly'), and a true throw (nothing saved, KEEP THE DRAFT so they can retry). Preserving the operator's typed message on failure is the difference between trust and rage.
strengths.evidence: handoff-detail-sheet.tsx:193-210 send(); :421-432 undelivered vs sendFailed banners

strengths.title: Copy is warm, plain-English, and de-jargoned at exactly the right moments
strengths.detail: Risk chips read 'Affects your ad spend or credits', 'Goes out to a client', 'Changes something outside Switchboard' instead of enum names; handoff reasons map 'max_turns_exceeded' → 'Conversation stalled', 'negative_sentiment' → 'Tone turned'; empty state is 'That's everything. Your team is on top of it.' This voice is the product's biggest trust asset.

strengths.title: Loading/error/empty are first-class, not afterthoughts — and the isError-before-empty ordering is guarded
strengths.detail: The handoff sheet has a shimmer skeleton AND a dedicated fetch-error card ('The connection dropped… The list is still safe — try again'). The inbox screen renders isError before empty so a failed load never masquerades as 'all clear', and there's an explicit code comment locking that ordering as a regression guard.
strengths.evidence: handoff-detail-sheet.tsx:66-99 skeleton+error; inbox-screen.tsx:176-179 'isError BEFORE empty'; inbox-error-state.tsx vs inbox-empty-state.tsx are deliberately distinct

issues.title: The Undo toast — the single safety net for irreversible spend/send — is an un-themed shadcn box that never auto-dismisses
issues.detail: Approving a financial or client-facing action surfaces only a toast carrying the Undo button. But that toast renders through the stock shadcn Toaster (generic Tailwind: 'rounded-md border bg-background p-6', slide-in-from-top), NOT the warm-editorial '.toast' system that inbox.css actually ships (dark ink, amber undo, branded). Worse, use-toast.ts hardcodes TOAST_REMOVE_DELAY = 1000000ms (~16.6 min) and no per-toast duration is passed, so the toast effectively never auto-dismisses, and TOAST_LIMIT=1 means a second approval silently evicts the first toast — destroying the Undo affordance for the first action before the user could use it. For a product whose whole promise is reversibility, the undo path is both visually off-brand and behaviorally broken.
issues.evidence: use-toast.ts:8-9 TOAST_LIMIT=1, TOAST_REMOVE_DELAY=1000000; toaster.tsx (generic shadcn); inbox.css:1361-1390 ships a beautiful .toast/.toast-undo that is DEAD; inbox-decision-item.tsx:42-52 toast({...action: Undo}) with no duration

issues.title: The approval detail sheet ships a hardcoded '—' placeholder grid labeled 'preview not yet wired' in production
issues.detail: Every approval you open to scrutinize shows a 'What this changes' section with Before '—', After '—', Confidence '—', Money at risk '—', a literal 'preview not yet wired' tag, and the caption 'We're saving a slot here for live before-and-after numbers — wiring it up next week.' This is the exact screen where a cautious owner goes to decide whether to trust spending their money — and it tells them the impact data is a TODO. It actively manufactures doubt at the highest-stakes moment, and dashed-ghost styling doesn't redeem shipping an empty promise.
issues.evidence: approval-detail-sheet.tsx:203-235 (unconditional ds-pending section, four hardcoded '—', 'preview not yet wired', 'wiring it up next week')

issues.title: The same Approve action has two different interaction models on Home vs Inbox
issues.detail: On Home, an approval renders as SwipeDecisionCard with INLINE Approve/Skip buttons + a ConfirmSheet bottom-sheet for needsConfirm. On Inbox, the identical decision renders as InboxDecisionCard — a 'doorway' with NO inline buttons; you must tap into a detail sheet where confirm is an INLINE expanding footer (ConfirmInline), not a sheet. Two different commit gestures, two different confirm UIs, two different button systems (CSS-module .btnPrimary vs global .ds-action-primary) for the same conceptual act. A time-poor owner who learns the gesture in one place is re-taught in the other; muscle memory never forms.
issues.evidence: needs-you-card.tsx:97-105 SwipeDecisionCard (inline btns); inbox-decision-card.tsx (doorway, no btns); ConfirmSheet (swipe-decision-card.tsx:193) vs ConfirmInline (approval-detail-sheet.tsx:42); two button systems: swipe-decision-card.module.css:.btnPrimary vs inbox.css:907 .ds-action-primary

issues.title: No in-flight feedback on commit — the card flies off 280ms BEFORE the mutation even fires, and there is no optimistic list update
issues.detail: commitApprove sets the exit animation and fires onApprove only after EXIT_MS=280ms; onApprove then awaits the network mutation with zero pending UI (no spinner, no disabled state on the flown card). The decision feed has no optimistic update — the row truly disappears only when invalidateQueries → refetch completes (feed also polls at 60s). So between swipe and refetch the user sees a card vanish on faith. If the mutation rejects, the toast is swallowed (.catch(()=>{})) and the card simply reappears on the next refetch with no explanation — a silent failure that reads as a ghost/bug. action.isPending is computed but never rendered.
issues.evidence: use-card-swipe.ts:119-123 commitApprove (animate then fire); use-decision-feed.ts:20-28 no optimistic, refetchInterval 60s; inbox-decision-item.tsx:35-56 (isPending guards re-entry but renders nothing; .catch swallows error)

issues.title: The 'already handled / expired' safety state is wired in the component but never passed in production
issues.detail: ApprovalDetailSheet has a complete alreadyHandled path — a banner plus disabled primary/secondary/dismiss buttons — but inbox-screen.tsx mounts ApprovalDetailItem WITHOUT ever computing or passing alreadyHandled. Because the feed is up to 60s stale, opening a card a teammate already actioned shows fully-live Approve buttons. Tapping them hits a 409 that the hook swallows as 'silent: true' and just closes the sheet with NO toast (handleCommit returns early on 'silent'). The user pressed Approve on a real-money action and got zero confirmation it did or didn't happen — the most anxiety-inducing possible outcome.
issues.evidence: approval-detail-sheet.tsx:77-89,128-133,288-314 (alreadyHandled fully built); inbox-screen.tsx:36-87 ApprovalDetailItem never passes alreadyHandled; use-recommendation-action.ts:24-27 (409 → silent); inbox-screen.tsx:47 'silent' → no toast

issues.title: Approval detail has no loading skeleton — only the handoff sheet does
issues.detail: HandoffDetailSheet fetches detail and shows a shimmer skeleton + fetch-error fallback. ApprovalDetailSheet renders purely from the already-loaded Decision, so on open it's instant — fine — but it means the two detail sheets feel inconsistent, and any future enrichment (the 'preview not yet wired' numbers) will pop in without a placeholder discipline. More immediately: the approval sheet's threadHref link is rendered but neutered with e.preventDefault(), so 'View conversation →' looks tappable and does nothing.
issues.evidence: handoff-detail-sheet.tsx:137-145 skeleton/error; approval-detail-sheet.tsx:262-270 ds-thread-link with onClick e.preventDefault() (dead link that looks live)

issues.title: Risk LEVEL pill and the actual swipe GATE can visibly disagree, with no explanation
issues.detail: The pill shows riskLevel ('low risk'), but swipe-approvability is governed by the four booleans. A decision can read 'low risk' yet still be un-swipeable (e.g. clientFacing:true) — so the card shows a reassuring 'low risk' pill AND a 'Tap to review' locked zone simultaneously. To the operator that's a quiet contradiction: why must I tap-review something you just called low risk? The reason (it goes to a client) is only discoverable two taps deep in the detail risk chips.
issues.evidence: inbox-decision-card.tsx:157-161 pill from riskLevel; :92-100 locked zone from canSwipeApprove (4 booleans); swipe-policy.ts:8-9

issues.title: Dead/contradictory SLA styling — 'near' state has CSS but is never produced
issues.detail: inbox.css and the detail sheet style data-due='near' (distinct weight/italic), but dueIn() only ever returns 'soon' | 'normal' | 'comfort' — 'near' is unreachable. The card also only flags data-near-sla on state==='soon', so the 5px urgency stripe and the heavier SLA type never actually trigger for the 'normal' middle band. The urgency ramp the design implies (comfort → normal → near → soon → overdue) collapses to essentially two visible states.
issues.evidence: time.ts:30-41 dueIn returns only soon/normal/comfort; inbox.css:298 .decision-sla[data-due='near'] (dead); inbox-decision-card.tsx:86 data-near-sla only on 'soon'

opportunities.title: Make Undo a real, branded, time-boxed safety bar
opportunities.detail: Replace the swallowed shadcn toast for commits with a purpose-built 'undo bar' that uses the warm .toast/.toast-undo styling inbox.css ALREADY ships, shows a visible countdown ring tied to the actual undoableUntil window (undoableFor() already computes it), and persists across a second approval (queue, don't evict). Pair it with optimistic list removal so the card leaves immediately and only restores-with-explanation on real failure. This single change turns the scariest moment (spending money) into the calmest.

opportunities.title: Unify the approve interaction into ONE model across Home and Inbox
opportunities.detail: Pick the doorway-or-swipe model and the confirm surface (sheet vs inline) once, and share a single ApproveButton/confirm component + one button token set. Today .btnPrimary (module) and .ds-action-primary (global) are two amber buttons maintained separately. Consolidating builds muscle memory and removes a whole class of 'why does this screen behave differently' friction.

opportunities.title: Replace the '—' placeholder grid with progressive disclosure that never shows an empty promise
opportunities.detail: Until the impact numbers are wired, render the dataLines and risk chips you DO have as the decision's substance, and simply omit the before/after grid rather than shipping 'preview not yet wired'. An owner should never see the product confess a TODO at the decision point. When the data lands, the grid earns its place.

opportunities.title: Show a real freshness/staleness signal and gate stale commits
opportunities.detail: Wire alreadyHandled (already built) by reconciling the open decision against the latest feed, and make 'Polling · checked just now' in the empty state honest everywhere (it's currently static copy). On a stale 409, show 'Already handled by [teammate] · 4m ago' instead of silently closing. Turns a ghost-bug moment into a moment of trust ('the system and my team are in sync').

opportunities.title: Reconcile the risk PILL with the GATE
opportunities.detail: When a 'low risk' item is still un-swipeable, say why on the card foot in one quiet line ('low risk · goes to a client → tap to review') instead of a bare 'Tap to review'. Removes the silent contradiction and teaches the model of why some low-risk things still need a look.

topMoves: Fix the Undo path end-to-end: brand it with the existing .toast-undo styling, give it a visible countdown bound to undoableUntil, stop it from auto-evicting on the next approval (TOAST_LIMIT=1 / 16-min delay), and add optimistic list removal so a commit feels instant and a failure explains itself instead of resurrecting a ghost card.

Delete the 'preview not yet wired' / '—' placeholder grid from the approval detail sheet before any operator sees it — render the data you have and omit what you don't; never confess a TODO at the moment someone is deciding to trust you with their money.
Unify Home and Inbox into ONE approve interaction model + one confirm surface + one amber-button token set, so the decisive gesture is identical everywhere and muscle memory forms.

Add real in-flight + staleness feedback: a pending state on commit (action.isPending is already computed), and wire the already-built alreadyHandled path so a stale 409 reads 'Already handled · 4m ago' instead of silently closing the sheet.

Resolve the 'low risk' pill vs 'Tap to review' lock contradiction with a one-line plain-English reason on the card, and clean up the dead SLA 'near' state so the urgency ramp the design promises is actually visible.

surface: Empty / loading / error / edge states (whole app)
feelVerdict: Two apps live in one repo. The agent-panel, Mira desk, and Results states are genuinely best-in-class — a documented "three-states-never-collapse" invariant, real block skeletons, error copy that never lies "0". But the Inbox — the single most trust-critical surface where a medspa owner approves spend and customer replies — is the weakest link: an unstyled bare "Loading…", a jarring layout shift, and a loading gate the team's own memory documents as broken that can flash "That's everything" (false inbox-zero) while data is still pending. The craft ceiling is extremely high; the floor is anxiety-inducing.

strengths.title: A documented, enforced "three-states-never-collapse" invariant in the agent panel
strengths.detail: open-decisions, work-log, and key-result each render a real skeleton on cold mount, an error state that explicitly never collapses to "0/nothing waiting", and a distinct empty state. The inline comments cite the exact failure they prevent ("flash error/empty during load"). This is the gold standard the rest of the app should be measured against.

strengths.title: Mira desk uses the CORRECT keys-pending gate and names the gotcha inline
strengths.detail: mira-desk-page computes `const pending = !desk && !deskQ.error` and comments "Gate on (!data && !error) — NOT isLoading — because a keys-pending query is disabled".
