This proves the team understands the React-Query trap; the bug is that the Inbox is an un-migrated outlier, not a knowledge gap.

strengths.title: Results states are honest, calm, and never failure-frame the happy path
strengths.detail: Distinct components for first-run ("Your first results land here once your team books a consult" — reassuring, not empty), no-Meta (degrades gracefully: campaigns empty but Alex revenue still shows), and a cache-age-aware error banner that shows the real staleness instead of pretending. renderBody() carefully refuses to show FirstRunNote on a fetch error (line 80) so "failed" never reads as "nothing yet".
strengths.evidence: src/components/results/states.tsx:46-82; src/components/results/results-page.tsx:80 ("Don't fall through to FirstRunNote — that would imply 'no data yet' rather than 'fetch failed'")

strengths.title: Home degrades per-module without ever fabricating numbers
strengths.detail: Each Home module is wrapped in a HomeModuleBoundary (a quiet inline "This section is unavailable." instead of a full-screen crash), and the composition refuses to promote the all-clear CALM layout on a feed error (isCalm requires decisionFeedAvailable). ThisWeek renders a "Your week's still being tallied" skeleton rather than inventing metrics. The decision feed uses the correct catch-all: `decisionFeedAvailable = !isError && data !== undefined`.

strengths.title: Inbox error state is firewalled from the empty state by design
strengths.detail: A dedicated InboxErrorState ("Couldn't load — connection dropped") renders BEFORE the empty branch, with a code comment flagging it as a "regression guard against isError mis-routing to the empty branch." An error and "all clear" must never look the same — exactly right for a trust surface.

issues.title: Inbox can flash "That's everything" (false inbox-zero) during session resolution
issues.detail: useDecisionFeed has `enabled: !!keys`, and useScopedQueryKeys returns null until the NextAuth session resolves with an organizationId. Per the team's own documented gotcha, a disabled query is pending+idle: isLoading=false, data=undefined, isError=false. The Inbox gate is `if (filtered.isLoading)` (line 181) — so during the keys-pending window that gate is SKIPPED, `decisions = filtered.data?.decisions ?? []` collapses to [], and the screen renders InboxEmptyState: a confident, beautifully-typeset "That's everything. Your team is on top of it." For an operator with pending approvals on the screen they trust to spend their money, briefly being told the queue is empty is the single most damaging false signal in the app. The Mira desk (mira-desk-page.tsx:34) already fixed this exact bug with `!desk && !deskQ.error`; the Inbox did not.
issues.evidence: src/components/inbox/inbox-screen.tsx:181 (`if (filtered.isLoading)`) + src/hooks/use-decision-feed.ts:27 (`enabled: !!keys`) + src/hooks/use-query-keys.ts:22 (returns null until orgId); contrast the correct fix at src/components/cockpit/mira/mira-desk-page.tsx:34

issues.title: Inbox loading state is an unstyled bare "Loading…" with a hard layout shift
issues.detail:
While the empty state is a polished card (44px icon medallion, 28px serif-italic heading, centered, card shadow) and the loaded view is a full editorial page (header + count + filter row + queue), the loading state is literally `
Loading…
`. The class `.inbox-loading` has NO CSS rule anywhere in inbox.css or inbox-design-base.css — it renders as default-font, top-left, unpadded text. So the first paint sequence for the app's core surface is: naked "Loading…" → (possibly false "That's everything") → full page snapping into place. Stripe/Linear/Mercury never show raw text where a skeleton belongs; this reads as a half-built screen.
issues.evidence: src/components/inbox/inbox-screen.tsx:182; grep for `.inbox-loading` in src/components/inbox/*.css returns zero rules (only `.inbox-empty` styles exist, inbox.css:401-447)

issues.title: A whole tier of surfaces ship bare unbranded text for loading AND error
issues.detail: Outside the agent-panel/Results islands, loading and error states are plain inline-styled sentences with no skeleton, no card, and (for errors) frequently no retry affordance. Mira creative detail: "Loading draft…", and on failure just "Couldn't load this draft — try again." with NO button to actually try again (the user's only recourse is a manual page reload). Mira desk error is a bare red line. Onboarding and operator panels are bare "Loading...". The result is that perceived quality swings wildly depending on which screen you land on — the product feels assembled by two different teams.
issues.evidence: src/app/(auth)/mira/creatives/[id]/creative-detail-page.tsx:19-22 (error has no retry button); src/components/cockpit/mira/mira-desk-page.tsx:69-73; src/app/(auth)/onboarding/page.tsx:130-138; src/app/(auth)/operator/_components/proposed-disqualifications-panel.tsx:13

issues.title: No route-level loading.tsx for any authenticated surface — navigation feels dead on slow links
issues.detail: The only loading.tsx in the app is for the public marketplace; the only error.tsx and not-found.tsx are global. The (auth) tree has zero per-route loading.tsx, so every authed screen relies on its own client-side hook gate. That means on navigation there is no instant skeleton shell — the destination renders blank/last-frame until its first hook fires (and on the Inbox, the first frame can be the broken state above). Linear/Vercel give an immediate skeleton on every route change; here the transition can feel like nothing happened.
issues.evidence: Only loading.tsx is src/app/(public)/loading.tsx; no src/app/(auth)/**/loading.tsx exists (find returned only the public one)

issues.title: Inbox empty-state "checked just now" can become quietly dishonest
issues.detail: InboxEmptyState hardcodes the footer "Polling · checked just now". The feed refetches every 60s, but if the network drops mid-session the empty card keeps asserting "checked just now" indefinitely — a small but real erosion of the calm-trust contract the rest of the app is so careful to protect (cf. the Results banner which shows real cacheAgeMinutes). A static reassurance that can outlive its truth is exactly the kind of thing this audience eventually notices.
issues.evidence: src/components/inbox/inbox-empty-state.tsx:37 ("Polling · checked just now" is a literal, not derived); contrast src/components/results/states.tsx:25-44 which uses real cacheAgeMinutes

opportunities.title: Promote the agent-panel "three-states" invariant to a shared, lint-enforced primitive
opportunities.detail: The pattern is already perfect in src/components/agent-panel and Mira desk. Extract a tiny <QueryStates loading={<Skeleton/>} error={...} empty={...}> wrapper (or a useQueryGate that returns 'loading'|'error'|'empty'|'data' using the !data && !error rule) and route every feed through it. This both kills the Inbox bug class and erases the bare-text tier in one move. The org already has a `<Skeleton>` primitive (src/components/ui/skeleton.tsx) but it's used in only ~6 files — there's a ready foundation.

opportunities.title: Design a real Inbox skeleton that mirrors the loaded layout
opportunities.detail:
Replace `
Loading…
` with a skeleton that paints the page header, the filter row, and 3-4 ghost decision rows at the real dimensions — so loading→loaded is a cross-fade, not a snap. This is the highest-visibility single screen in the product; it deserves the same care Results already got (ResultsSkeleton at states.tsx:64).

opportunities.title: Add loading.tsx skeleton shells for the core authed routes (inbox, results, mira)
opportunities.detail: Even a layout-matched skeleton at the route level makes every navigation feel instant and intentional, and it gives Next a Suspense fallback so the destination never paints its broken-first-frame. Pair each with the matching client skeleton so SSR and CSR agree.

opportunities.title: Give every error state a recovery affordance and a calm voice
opportunities.detail: Mira creative-detail error and Mira desk error are dead-ends (no retry). Standardize on the InboxErrorState / Results ErrorBanner shape: one warm sentence + a real "Try again" button wired to refetch. No screen should leave a non-technical operator with reload-the-page as their only option.

topMoves: Fix the Inbox false-empty: change the gate from `if (filtered.isLoading)` to the keys-pending-safe `if (!filtered.data && !filtered.isError)` (the exact fix Mira desk already ships at mira-desk-page.tsx:34) so an operator with pending approvals can never be told "That's everything" during session resolution.

Replace the unstyled `inbox-loading` bare text with a real Inbox skeleton (page header + filter row + 3-4 ghost decision rows at loaded dimensions) so the core surface cross-fades instead of snapping — matching the quality of ResultsSkeleton.

Extract the agent-panel's documented three-states invariant into one shared <QueryStates>/useQueryGate primitive and route every feed (Inbox, Mira detail, Mira desk, operator, knowledge) through it, eliminating both the isLoading gotcha class and the bare-text tier in a single change.
Add layout-matched loading.tsx shells for /inbox, /results, and /mira so every authed navigation feels instant and never paints a broken first frame.

Give the dead-end error states (Mira creative detail at creative-detail-page.tsx:20-21, Mira desk) a real "Try again" button wired to refetch, and derive the Inbox empty-state's "checked just now" from the real fetch time so no reassurance outlives its truth.

surface: Content, voice & microcopy (whole app) — agent greetings, Home verdict, Inbox decision cards + approval/handoff sheets, Results, agent panels, Mira creative desk, Settings, onboarding/login, landing, and the global halt + operator-chat surfaces.
feelVerdict: When the voice is "on," this is some of the best trust-copy I've seen in an operator product: greetings, error states, and money/destructive confirms read like a calm, honest colleague who never blames you and never overclaims ("Saved — but we couldn't deliver it... nothing was saved" is genuinely Stripe-grade). But the voice is uneven: it's authored beautifully in the new warm-editorial surfaces (Home, Inbox, Results, Mira) and falls off a cliff in the older/utility surfaces — a globally-mounted blue "Operator Chat: Type a command" widget and a bare "Spend Limits / Daily Limit ($)" form undercut the whole "trustworthy colleague" promise on screens that matter most for money and autonomy. A handful of "preview not yet wired / wiring it up next week" admissions sit inside the highest-stakes approval sheet, which quietly erodes confidence right at the decision moment.

strengths.title: Failure microcopy distinguishes failure modes precisely and never blames the operator
strengths.detail: The handoff composer separates 'saved but undelivered' from 'send failed, nothing saved' — the exact distinction an anxious operator needs to know whether to re-send. This is the gold standard for trust copy and should be the template for every mutation in the app.
strengths.evidence: src/components/inbox/handoff-detail-sheet.tsx:422-431 ('Saved — but we couldn't deliver it to {leadFirstName} right now...' vs 'We couldn't send that just now — nothing was saved. Check your connection and try again.')

strengths.title: Money + destructive confirms state the irreversible consequence in plain words before the click
strengths.detail: Mira's continue/stop confirm names the provider cost and the irreversibility explicitly, in lowercase human language, not legalese. 'This may create provider cost (about $X)' and 'Stop this draft? You can't continue it later. This can't be undone.' is exactly the calm-but-honest register the audience needs around spend.

strengths.title: Agent personas are differentiated by sentence shape, not by costume — Alex warm, Riley numerical, Mira draft-only
strengths.detail: The greeting projection encodes voice as structure: Alex says 'Maya is the one I'd answer first.', Riley says 'needs your eye first.', Mira says 'never published without you.' Personality comes through without a single emoji or cutesy tic, and Mira's copy repeatedly reassures 'never published without you' / 'You'll review the draft before anything goes further.'

strengths.title: First-person work log reads like a colleague's standup, with a hard honesty guardrail against invented time anchors
strengths.detail: composeActivityVoice renders 'I booked Maya's consult...', 'I escalated Maya to you —...', and the WorkLog header deliberately refuses to say 'since you last looked' because the wire has no last-viewed timestamp. Choosing factual-but-warm over fabricated-but-cozy is the right instinct and is documented in-code.

strengths.title: Results + Inbox empty/error states are honest, non-alarming, and never collapse 'error' into 'all clear'
strengths.detail: FirstRunNote reassures ('Riley and Alex are already running — check back after your next booking'), the error banner owns the failure without blame ('We couldn't reach your data stores. Showing the last cached pull from N minutes ago.'), and the inbox error explicitly refuses to reuse the empty-state 'all clear' copy.

strengths.title: Landing voice nails the positioning thesis in one line
strengths.detail: 'A revenue desk you hire one seat at a time' + 'Setup in a day · Agents draft, you publish · Stays in your control' communicates the entire trust+autonomy model crisply, matching the in-app 'you publish' promise. Voice is consistent from marketing into the product.

issues.title: Global 'Operator Chat — Type a command' widget is a total voice + visual register break, on every authed screen
issues.detail: The operator-chat widget is mounted in the authed layout, so it floats on Home, Inbox, Results, Settings — everywhere. Its copy is command-line jargon ('Type a command like "show pipeline" or "pause low-performing ads"', placeholder 'Type a command...', header 'Operator Chat', button 'Chat'), and it's styled in raw Tailwind bg-blue-600/hover:bg-blue-700 — a color that exists nowhere in the warm-amber design system. For a non-technical, anxious medspa owner this reframes the whole product from 'trustworthy colleague' to 'developer console you must learn commands for.' It contradicts the personas (Alex/Riley/Mira) the rest of the app spent so much care building.
issues.evidence: src/app/(auth)/layout.tsx:22 (mounted globally); src/components/operator-chat/operator-chat-widget.tsx:41 (bg-blue-600), :44 ('Chat'/'Close'), :52 ('Operator Chat'), :59-60 ('Type a command like "show pipeline"...'), :75 ('Type a command...')

issues.title: The highest-stakes approval sheet admits its own unfinished plumbing right at the decision moment
issues.detail: On the approval detail sheet — the screen where an operator decides to spend money or send something to a customer — the 'What this changes' section renders Before/After/Confidence/Money-at-risk as em-dashes with a literal 'preview not yet wired' tag and the line 'We're saving a slot here for live before-and-after numbers — wiring it up next week.' Honesty is good, but exposing internal roadmap ('next week', 'not yet wired') in the trust-critical moment reads as 'this product isn't finished,' and 'Money at risk: —' is the single worst cell to show blank on a spend decision. Either compute it or hide the section until it's real.
issues.evidence: src/components/inbox/approval-detail-sheet.tsx:203-235 ('preview not yet wired', 'Money at risk' value '—', 'wiring it up next week')

issues.title: Spend Limits — the core autonomy/money-control surface — is generic, Title-Cased, and emotionally cold
issues.detail: This is where an owner sets how much an AI may spend without asking — it should be the most reassuring screen in the app. Instead it's a bare shadcn Card titled 'Spend Limits' with four inputs labeled 'Daily Limit ($)', 'Weekly Limit ($)', 'Per Action Limit ($)', placeholder 'No limit', and a button that just says 'Save'. No framing of what happens when a limit is hit, no 'Alex will ask you before spending more than this', no reassurance. 'No limit' as the default placeholder is actively anxiety-inducing for this audience. Title Case + 'Save' also breaks the lowercase-editorial voice used elsewhere.
issues.evidence: src/components/settings/spend-limits-form.tsx:39 ('Spend Limits'), :45-50 ('Daily Limit ($)'), :56 (placeholder 'No limit'), :65 ('Save')

issues.title: Two parallel agent-role vocabularies will confuse — 'Lead response / Ad optimizer / Creative' vs the registry's 'lead-to-speed / ad-optimizer / creative'
issues.detail: The agent panel hard-codes display roles (Alex 'Lead response', Riley 'Ad optimizer', Mira 'Creative') in one file, while the schema registry stores different role strings and the greeting config uses count-nouns ('leads'/'ad sets'/'drafts'). 'Creative' as a standalone role label is a thin, slightly off noun for the audience ('Creative director' or 'Creative & content' would read warmer and clearer). Because roles live in 3+ places, they will drift; the panel role copy is also the least considered of the three.
issues.evidence: src/components/agent-panel/lib/agent-display.ts:7-9 ('Lead response'/'Ad optimizer'/'Creative'); packages/schemas/src/agents.ts:7,15,23 (role: 'lead-to-speed'/'ad-optimizer'/'creative')

issues.title: Inbox decision-card affordance verbs are slightly inconsistent and one is ambiguous
issues.detail: On a single card the swipe-reveal zone says 'Send'/'Skip'/'Snooze' while the foot affordance says 'Swipe →' / 'Tap to open →' / 'Tap to review →', and the locked zone says 'Tap to review' but the approval sheet's primary is the producer's label + '…'. 'Skip' on an approval is also semantically fuzzy for money decisions — it reads as 'ignore' rather than 'decide later,' which is risky copy when the thing skipped is a spend approval. Tighten to one verb per outcome (e.g. 'Approve / Review / Later').
issues.evidence: src/components/inbox/inbox-decision-card.tsx:90-100 ('Snooze'/'Skip'/'Send'/'Tap to review'), :167-174 ('Swipe →'/'Tap to open →'/'Tap to review →')

issues.title: 'Coming soon' appears as a real user-facing status for Mira in the agent panel
issues.detail: When Mira isn't enabled, the panel shows 'Mira isn't set up yet / She becomes available as your workspace grows' with a 'Coming soon' meta tag. 'Coming soon' is marketing-placeholder language that reads as vaporware inside a paid product; 'She becomes available as your workspace grows' is also vague about what the operator must actually do. State the concrete unlock condition or remove the tag.
issues.evidence: src/components/agent-panel/mira-panel.tsx:38-42 ('Mira isn't set up yet', 'Coming soon')

opportunities.title: Re-skin the operator-chat widget into an agent-voiced 'Ask Alex' affordance (or remove it pre-launch)
opportunities.detail: Replace 'Operator Chat / Type a command' with the warm register: trigger labeled 'Ask Alex', placeholder 'Ask about your leads, ads, or results…', empty-state 'Ask me anything — try "how are my ads doing?" or "who needs a reply?"', and amber/agent-hue styling instead of bg-blue-600. If natural-language isn't truly wired, hide it behind a flag rather than shipping command-line jargon globally. Highest single-screen consistency win in the app.

opportunities.title: Turn Spend Limits into a trust moment with consequence-framing microcopy
opportunities.detail: Reframe as 'How much can Alex spend before asking you?' with per-field helper text ('Above this in a day, I'll pause and ask before spending more') and a plain-English summary line that reads back the active policy ('Right now: Alex can spend up to $X/day on ads without checking in.'). Replace 'No limit' default with an explicit 'Always ask me' option. This converts the scariest settings screen into the most reassuring one.

opportunities.title: Replace roadmap-leak placeholders in the approval sheet with either real numbers or graceful absence
opportunities.detail: Drop 'preview not yet wired' / 'wiring it up next week' from the trust-critical surface. If before/after and money-at-risk can't be computed for an item, omit the 'What this changes' block entirely (the risk chips already carry weight) rather than rendering blank '—' cells with an apology. Never show 'Money at risk: —' on a spend decision.

opportunities.title: Publish a tiny voice + lexicon guide and centralize the canonical verbs
opportunities.detail: Codify the rules already implicit in the good copy (lowercase editorial headers; never blame the user; name the irreversible consequence; never fabricate a time anchor; one verb per outcome) and a single source of truth for action verbs (Approve / Review / Later / Decline) + agent roles, so Settings/operator-chat/legacy Mercury surfaces stop drifting. The strong surfaces already embody this — it just needs to be written down and enforced.

opportunities.title: Tighten inbox outcome verbs and replace 'Skip' on money approvals
opportunities.detail: Standardize the doorway-card lexicon to one verb per outcome and swap 'Skip' for 'Later' or 'Decide later' on approvals so an operator never feels they're silently ignoring a spend decision. Keep 'Snooze' for handoffs only. Small change, meaningful reduction in decision anxiety.

topMoves: Re-skin or flag-hide the globally-mounted operator-chat widget — kill 'Operator Chat / Type a command / bg-blue-600' and either voice it as 'Ask Alex' in the warm register or remove it until NL is real. It's the biggest voice+visual break and it's on every authed screen.

Rewrite Spend Limits with consequence-framing microcopy ('How much can Alex spend before asking you?' + 'Above this, I'll pause and ask' + a plain-English policy readback). Turn the scariest money/autonomy screen into the most reassuring one.

Remove the 'preview not yet wired' / 'wiring it up next week' / 'Money at risk: —' placeholders from the approval detail sheet; show real numbers or omit the block. Don't expose unfinished plumbing at the moment of a spend/customer decision.

Write a one-page voice + lexicon guide that codifies what the best surfaces already do (lowercase editorial, never blame the user, name the irreversible consequence, never fabricate time anchors) and centralize action verbs + agent roles so Settings and legacy surfaces stop drifting.

Standardize inbox outcome verbs to one-per-outcome and replace 'Skip' with 'Later' on approvals, plus fix 'Coming soon'/'Creative' role copy — small consistency fixes that compound into a more trustworthy, finished feel.

surface: Emotional design, delight & intangible feel — across Home, Inbox, Results, agent cockpit/panel, the agent-as-character system, and the v6 marketing landing (apps/dashboard).
feelVerdict: At its best — the "This Week" signed letter, the Results "— Riley" pullquote, the warm honest empty states, the v6 landing — this feels like a genuinely premium, trustworthy editorial colleague; you'd hand it your ad budget. But the everyday surface (Home + Inbox) is calmer than it is alive: it loads fully static with no entrance, the most-touched screens render agents as flat letter-discs while the one beautiful breathing character is buried in Settings, and a bare unstyled "Loading…" flash on the Inbox punctures the spell. The craft ceiling is top-decile; the floor is inconsistent, and the soul is mostly offstage.

strengths.title: The week-note: an employee's letter, not a dashboard widget
strengths.detail: This is the single most love-worthy moment in the app. Alex writes a warm, first-person 'week-so-far note' — mono eyebrow 'ALEX · week-so-far note', 'Mon → today', an editorial serif drop-cap on the first letter tinted in the agent's identity hue, prose composed only from real numbers, and a signed monogram '— Alex' signoff above a 'Read full report →' link. It makes the AI feel like a colleague who reports to you, which is exactly the trust posture a time-poor medspa owner needs. Nothing about it feels like SaaS.
strengths.evidence: components/home/this-week.tsx + this-week.module.css:4 (.weeknote 'an employee email, not a dashboard'), :96 (.weeknoteBody.dropcap::first-letter)

strengths.title: Editorial agent voice with a byline earns trust
strengths.detail: Results opens with a pull-quote verdict signed '— Riley' (verdict-line.tsx:18), and the Home verdict is one honest serif sentence that 'earns the screen' ('One thing needs you. Alex has it ready.' / 'All caught up. Your team's running clean.'). Attributing judgment to a named agent, in a confident editorial register, is far more reassuring than an anonymous metric. The verdict even degrades gracefully to 'Your team is on shift.' when signals are unavailable — calm, never alarming.

strengths.title: Honest, warm, non-failure-framed empty/error states
strengths.detail: Every degraded state is written by someone who cares about the operator's nerves. Inbox empty: 'That's everything. Your team is on top of it.' with 'Polling · checked just now'. Results first-run: 'Your first results land here once your team books a consult. Riley and Alex are already running.' Results error: 'Showing the last cached pull from N minutes ago' (real cache age, never faked). Home overnight: 'All quiet overnight.' This honesty IS the product's emotional moat — it never pretends, never blames, never invents a number.

strengths.title: The inbox decision card is a calm 'doorway', and the detail sheet is genuinely premium
strengths.detail: The queue card is deliberately quiet — serif lede (humanSummary), hairline risk pill that is 'never a traffic light', a 3px agent identity stripe, no shouty inline buttons; the whole card is a tap-target into a rich detail sheet. The sheet has a real conversation thread with agent/lead bubbles, a featured one-tap 'suggested opening', a ghosted dashed 'What this changes — pending' block that is present-but-honestly-unfilled, and an inline confirm step with a note field. Swipe-to-send reveals an 'Send' zone with a satisfying fly-off + collapse. This is Linear/Superhuman-tier interaction design for the highest-stakes moment.

strengths.title: Disciplined color semantics: amber = the one action, agent hue = identity only
strengths.detail: The product enforces a strict three-layer rule everywhere — cream base, agent color for orientation, a single operator-amber for every action — and it shows. Buttons are amber with a tactile inset/lift shadow; agent coral/teal/violet only ever tint avatars, names, and stripes, never a control. This restraint is what makes the surface feel calm and trustworthy rather than like a noisy dashboard, and it is consistently honored across Home, Inbox, and Results.
strengths.evidence: home.module.css:451-489 (.btnPrimary amber-only comment 'identity color may NEVER back an action button'); inbox-decision-card.tsx:43 LOCKED RULE 4

strengths.title: The marketing landing nails the emotional thesis
strengths.detail: The v6 hero copy is the clearest expression of the whole product's promise: 'A revenue desk you hire one seat at a time', 'Hire one. Or hire the desk — they share context as they go', and the reassurance line 'Setup in a day · Agents draft, you publish · Stays in your control'. The agent-name headline crossfades on toggle, mono beat-frame labels ('01 — Meet the desk') give it an editorial film-credit cadence, and 'they share context as they go' sells the multi-agent magic without hype. A medspa owner reads this and feels capable, not threatened.

issues.title: The soul is offstage: the one beautiful breathing character lives only in Settings; Home shows agents as flat letter-discs
issues.detail: OperatorCharacter is a lovingly built, alive component — a soft organic SVG figure that floats (character-float), has a breathing radial aura (aura-breathe), changes posture/tone/autonomy via CSS transforms, and pulses on config change. It is the single most 'alive', characterful, premium-feeling asset in the entire codebase. And it renders on exactly ONE screen users rarely visit: Settings → Identity. On Home — the daily screen, the emotional anchor — the agents are represented by a 30px circle with a single capital letter ({name[0]}) in the Team Pulse ribbon, and the hero (Verdict) renders NO agent face at all. The product's warmth peaks where nobody looks and flatlines where everybody lives. This is the biggest gap between the product's potential feel and its actual feel.
issues.evidence: components/character/operator-character.tsx (only importer: app/(auth)/settings/identity/page.tsx); home/team-pulse.tsx:39-40 ({name[0]} disc); home-page.tsx hero = Verdict, which renders no avatar

issues.title: Five competing agent-visual systems make identity feel incoherent across surfaces
issues.detail: An agent's 'face' is drawn five different ways depending on where you are: (1) the breathing OperatorCharacter on Settings; (2) animated pixel-art sprites for Alex/Riley (Mira has none) in the Inbox cards and cockpit chips via InboxAgentAvatar/SpriteChip; (3) geometric glyph marks (Alex=radar lines, Riley=arrow, Mira=orbit) rendered in muted gray on login + onboarding via AgentMark; (4) a plain initial-letter disc on Home and as the sprite fallback; (5) an unrendered 96–240px editorial '.portrait' circle still sitting in globals.css. A user moving Home → Inbox → Settings → onboarding sees the same agent as a letter, then a pixel creature, then a breathing blob, then a gray radar glyph. There is no single memorable 'this is Alex' image to fall in love with — the character equity is split five ways and adds up to none.
issues.evidence: operator-character.tsx; cockpit/sprite/sprite-chip.tsx + inbox/inbox-agent-avatar.tsx:29-33; character/agent-mark.tsx:104-125 (importers: login + 6 onboarding files); home/team-pulse.tsx:40; globals.css:751 (.portrait, no live renderer)

issues.title: Home is motion-dead on first paint — the 'alive' promise is unmet on the primary screen
issues.detail: home.module.css contains zero @keyframes and no entrance/reveal/fade — every module appears instantly and sits perfectly still. There is no staggered entrance, no number count-up on the metric, no settle on the verdict line, no scroll-reveal (the use-scroll-reveal and use-entrance-played hooks exist in the codebase but are not used on Home). The only motion you will ever see on the daily screen is a 1px hover-lift on a chip and the header's live-pip ring. For a product whose pitch is a living 'desk' of colleagues working for you, the home screen feels like a printed page, not a place where something is happening. Calm is right; inert is not.
issues.evidence: home.module.css (grep '@keyframes' → none); hooks/use-scroll-reveal.ts + use-entrance-played.ts exist but are not imported by components/home/*

issues.title: Bare unstyled 'Loading…' flashes on every Inbox open — a hole in an otherwise polished surface
issues.detail:
InboxScreen's loading branch returns
Loading…
, and 'inbox-loading' has no CSS rule anywhere — so it renders as raw 16px left-aligned default-font black text on the cream page, with no skeleton, no masthead, no shimmer. This is jarring precisely because the rest of the app is so considered: the inbox detail sheet has gorgeous .ds-sk shimmer skeletons, and Results has warm block-skeletons. The very first thing a returning operator sees when they tap into their decision queue is the cheapest-looking moment in the product. First impressions of the highest-frequency surface matter most.
issues.evidence: components/inbox/inbox-screen.tsx:182; grep 'inbox-loading' across all CSS → no rule; contrast inbox.css:1318-1332 (.ds-sk shimmer), results/states.tsx:64-82 (warm skeleton)

issues.title: Three token systems with non-identical agent colors and a documented history of collision bugs
issues.detail: Agent identity colors are defined three times in three formats with values that do NOT match: globals.css --agent-alex = hsl(14 70% 58%); the inbox redeclares --coral = #e07856 (a different coral); and the cockpit uses a hardcoded-hex 'T' object plus per-agent accent constants. Mira is hsl(270 45% 58%) in globals but #7e6bb2 (--violet) in the inbox, with yet another MIRA_ACCENT #4A3A66/#E7E1F0 in inbox-agent-avatar.tsx. The inbox-design-base.css and inbox-screen tests carry long comments narrating past bugs where the triplet/literal mismatch made cards render transparent and swipe zones bleed through. Visually the deltas are subtle, but the fragmentation is a latent feel-risk (the same agent is a slightly different color on different screens) and a real maintenance/consistency tax that will keep producing 'it looks broken' regressions.
issues.evidence: globals.css:62-64 vs inbox-design-base.css:30-38 (#e07856/#7e6bb2) vs cockpit/tokens.ts + inbox-agent-avatar.tsx:24-27 (MIRA_ACCENT)

issues.title: ~570 lines of richer, abandoned editorial Home CSS still ship — and hint the design got downgraded
issues.detail: globals.css carries a fully-realized earlier 'Alex Home' aesthetic — .greeting-block with a 240px portrait, a giant 140px .hero-num, an animated .sparkline, a horizontally-scrolling .pipeline of lead .tiles, .win cards — and not one of those classes (greeting-prose, hero-num, win-prose, tile-name, pipeline-scroll) is rendered by any live .tsx. Beyond being dead weight in the global stylesheet, it is evidence that the more cinematic, portrait-led, big-number Home was replaced by today's quieter letter-disc Home. The current Home is more honest and modular, but it traded away the wow. Worth a deliberate decision: delete the corpse, or harvest its best moments (the hero number, the portrait) back into the live Home.
issues.evidence: globals.css:729-1191 (.greeting-block/.portrait/.hero-num/.sparkline/.pipeline/.win/.tile); grep of each class in *.tsx → 0 renderers

issues.title: No celebratory release after approving something that spends money or talks to a customer
issues.detail: The emotional peak of this product is the instant an operator approves the agent to spend their budget or message their client — that act of trust deserves a beat of confident, reversible closure. Today it resolves to a standard toast ('Approved · Sent for {name}' with an Undo) and the card flies off. The toast and fly-off are clean, but there is no signature micro-moment of relief/satisfaction (no soft check bloom, no agent acknowledgement, no 'handled' settle) that would make a nervous owner feel good about having delegated. The reversibility (Undo) is excellent for trust; the delight on commit is missing.
issues.evidence: components/inbox/inbox-decision-item.tsx:42-50 (toast on approve); inbox.css:476-485 (fly-off) — no celebratory keyframe beyond translate+collapse

opportunities.title: Promote one canonical agent portrait to Home and the agent panel — bring the soul onstage
opportunities.detail: Pick ONE agent representation as canonical (the breathing OperatorCharacter is the strongest candidate — it already encodes role/tone/autonomy and breathes) and render a small, calm version of it in the Home verdict hero and the AgentPanel header instead of letter-discs/sprites. Even a 40–56px breathing mark next to 'Alex has it ready' would make the daily screen feel alive and make the agent feel present. The asset already exists and is performance-conscious (gradient aura, not blur; pauses on hidden tab). This is the highest impact-to-effort move in the whole audit.

opportunities.title: Give Home a 600–900ms editorial entrance and a single number count-up
opportunities.detail: Add a gentle staggered fade-up (the fade-in keyframe and ease-home curve already exist) so the verdict, then the cards, then the quiet rail settle in sequence on load — and count the one hero metric up from 0. This converts the page from 'printed' to 'arriving' without sacrificing calm. The use-scroll-reveal/use-entrance-played hooks already exist; wire them. Respect prefers-reduced-motion (already globally handled).

opportunities.title: Replace the bare Inbox 'Loading…' with a masthead-preserving skeleton
opportunities.detail: Render the 'inbox' masthead + filter row immediately (they don't depend on data) and show 2–3 .ds-sk-style shimmer card placeholders below, reusing the shimmer already defined in inbox.css. The returning operator should always see their queue's frame instantly, never a naked system-font 'Loading…'. Closes the single most-visible polish hole.

opportunities.title: Unify agent identity into one token source and one avatar component
opportunities.detail: Collapse the three color definitions to a single source of truth (define agent hues once, consume everywhere) so #e07856 and hsl(14 70% 58%) become one value, and standardize on a single <AgentAvatar> that internally chooses sprite/portrait/initial. This removes the latent 'same agent, different color on different screens' incoherence and retires the collision-bug class of regressions documented in the inbox CSS comments.

opportunities.title: Add a small, reversible celebration to the approve moment
opportunities.detail: On commit, before the fly-off, play a brief agent-tinted confirmation — a soft check bloom or the agent mark giving a single acknowledging pulse, paired with the existing 'Approved · Undo' toast. Keep it under ~400ms and amber/agent-tinted so it reads as 'your colleague handled it', reinforcing both delight and the trust loop at the product's emotional peak.

opportunities.title: Either delete or harvest the abandoned editorial Home CSS
opportunities.detail: Make a call on the ~570 dead lines (greeting-block/portrait/hero-num/sparkline/pipeline/win/tile). If the quieter Home is final, delete them to keep globals.css honest. If the cinematic version is missed, selectively reintroduce the giant hero number and the portrait into the live modular Home — those two elements alone would restore most of the 'wow' the current Home traded away.

topMoves: Bring the soul onstage: render a small breathing agent portrait (reuse OperatorCharacter) in the Home verdict hero and the AgentPanel header, replacing the flat letter-discs — the most love-worthy asset currently hides in Settings.

Make Home arrive, not just appear: add a 600–900ms staggered editorial entrance + a single hero-number count-up (the fade-in keyframe, ease-home curve, and reveal hooks already exist), so the daily screen feels alive while staying calm.

Kill the cheapest moment in the product: replace the bare unstyled Inbox 'Loading…' with a masthead-preserving shimmer skeleton (reuse the existing .ds-sk shimmer) so the highest-frequency surface never looks broken on entry.

Unify agent identity to one color source and one <AgentAvatar> component, so Alex isn't #e07856 in the inbox and hsl(14 70% 58%) on Home — ending the documented token-collision regressions and making identity feel coherent across surfaces.

Add a sub-400ms reversible celebration on approve (agent-tinted check bloom / acknowledging pulse) at the product's emotional peak — the instant an operator trusts an agent with their money or their customer.

surface: Responsive + desktop >=1024 tier coherence (apps/dashboard) — shell, nav, Home bento, Inbox two-pane, detail sheets across phone/tablet/desktop
feelVerdict: On a phone it feels genuinely native and calm — the editorial column, the bottom tab bar, and the slide-up sheets read like a thoughtfully-built mobile app. But it is two products wearing one skin: the moment you cross 1024px the warm editorial nav is swapped for a generic shadcn icon-sidebar, and the tablet band (768-1023) is an awkward in-between where a 480px detail rail and a bottom-anchored agent sheet fight on the same screen. The desktop tier is competent and well-commented, but it reads as "mobile, then bolted-on desktop," not one system that breathes across sizes.

strengths.title: Desktop frame is calm and correctly capped, not stretched
strengths.detail: The whole app (sidebar + content) centers in a 1280px --app-frame with generous 56px insets at >=1280, so a 1440/1920 monitor gets restful margins instead of full-bleed sprawl. The Home main reading column lands around 500px and the bento rail is a fixed 320px — a deliberate measure, not a fluid stretch. This is the single best responsive decision in the slice.

strengths.title: Inbox two-pane docking is engineered with real cascade discipline
strengths.detail: The master-detail flip reuses the SAME .sheet element as the mobile bottom-sheet, re-anchoring it from fixed-bottom to in-grid sticky at lg, with the scrim removed in two-pane mode. The specificity math (.app-main scoping to beat later imports, re-capping max-height against auto-height ancestors) is documented inline and defends against the known global-CSS cascade hazard. When it renders, it will feel like Superhuman/Linear.

strengths.title: useIsDesktop resolves synchronously to avoid a hydration/animation flash
strengths.detail: The hook reads matchMedia inside the useState initializer so the first client render already knows the breakpoint — meaning the AgentPanel can pick its slide direction (bottom vs right) without a post-effect flip. The docstring even reasons about which consumers are hydration-safe. This is a notch above the typical useEffect-only media hook.

strengths.title: Type and spacing scale up at breakpoints, not just reflow
strengths.detail: The editorial voice grows with the viewport: the Home verdict line goes 36px->48px, the calm hero 40px->56px, the Inbox masthead 36px->44px, decision lede 19.5px->21px. The screen doesn't just get wider, it gets more confident — the right instinct for an editorial product.

strengths.title: Reduced-motion and safe-area are respected in the responsive chrome
strengths.detail: The fixed bottom nav and docked action rows pad for env(safe-area-inset-bottom), and a global prefers-reduced-motion block neutralizes animations — so the responsive furniture behaves on notched phones and for motion-sensitive operators.

issues.title: Desktop nav is a different design language than the rest of the app
issues.detail: Below 1024 the primary nav is warm editorial: Source-Serif text labels, an amber underline on the active item, only Home/Inbox/Results. At >=1024 that whole nav is hidden (.hide-at-lg) and replaced by AppSidebar — a generic shadcn icon list (lucide icons, text-[13.5px], bg-surface active chip, hover:bg-surface/50) that also surfaces a different IA (Pipeline, Automations, Mira, Full reports, Settings). So the desktop user navigates a plainer, icon-driven, more-crowded menu that looks like a different app than the phone. The product's stated 'text-only underline nav, calm editorial' identity simply does not survive the jump to desktop.
issues.evidence: editorial-auth-shell.tsx:36-37 (PrimaryNav wrapped in .hide-at-lg) vs app-sidebar.tsx:79-92 (NavLink shadcn classes) and :33-59 (richer IA); primary-nav CSS at globals.css:1236-1282

issues.title: The same 'open a detail' gesture behaves differently per surface on tablet
issues.detail: On a 768-1023px tablet, opening an agent panel from Home yields a bottom slide-up sheet (AgentPanel uses side = isDesktop?'right':'bottom', and isDesktop is gated at 1024 so tablets read as mobile), but opening a decision in Inbox yields a 480px right-rail sheet (.sheet flips to right at 768). Two conceptually identical 'drill into detail' actions animate from two different edges on the very same device. There is no single 'detail surface' model — each feature picked its own breakpoint (768 vs 1024) and its own anchor.
issues.evidence: agent-panel.tsx:75,90 (useIsDesktop @1024 -> bottom on tablet) vs inbox-design-base.css:97-113 (.sheet -> right rail @768)

issues.title: Five competing breakpoints with no shared system
issues.detail: Across the slice, layout decisions fire at 560 (Results grids), 768 (column cap, inbox sheet, nav inline), 1024 (sidebar, bento, inbox two-pane, agent panel), 1280 (header/body inset, page padding, section rhythm), and 1440 (dashboard-frame max). The 1024-vs-1280 split is the worst offender: at >=1024 the sidebar+content grid turns on, but the header row and body only get their wide 56px inset at >=1280 — so between 1024 and 1279 the content sits at one inset while the desktop chrome is already active, and the header's max-width (1280) and body frame (1280) reach alignment only above 1280. There is no tokenized breakpoint ladder; every file improvises.
issues.evidence: results.module.css:260,797,838 (560); globals.css:502-511 (header 768/1280), :517-541 (body grid @1024, inset @1280), :352 (1440); use-is-desktop.ts (1024)

issues.title: Fixed bottom nav can occlude the last content on phone — and only Inbox compensated
issues.detail: The mobile primary-nav is position:fixed;bottom:0 (~54px + safe-area), but there is no global bottom-inset on .app-content/.app-main. Home's and Results' .column set only 28px bottom padding, so the final element (Home's Permissions 'Adjust' link / Results' Colophon) scrolls under the tab bar on phones. Tellingly, Inbox DID add padding-bottom:96px — proving the author hit this and patched one surface, while Home and Results were left exposed. There is no shared --bottom-nav-clearance token, so the fix is inconsistent by construction.
issues.evidence: globals.css:1211-1224 (fixed nav, no host inset); home.module.css:14 and results.module.css:1252 (28px) vs inbox-design-base.css:173 (96px)

issues.title: Inbox permanently reserves a 480px detail pane, squeezing the list narrower than the detail
issues.detail: At >=1024 the inbox grid is minmax(0,1fr) 480px and the right column is ALWAYS occupied — when nothing is selected, a dashed 'Select an item to see details' placeholder holds the 480px open. Inside the 1280 frame (minus sidebar 216, gaps, insets) the list column collapses to roughly 400px, i.e. the detail pane is WIDER than the list of things needing you. The triage list — the actual job — gets the smaller half of the screen even at rest. A medspa owner scanning approvals on a laptop sees a cramped list beside a big empty prompt box.
issues.evidence: inbox-design-base.css:201-213 (480px reserved), :239-251 (.inbox-detail-empty always sticky/visible); inbox-screen.tsx:220-224

issues.title: Two desktop-detection mechanisms with different flash behavior
issues.detail: AgentPanel uses useIsDesktop() (synchronous, no flash), but results-page.tsx runs its OWN useState('mobile')+useEffect matchMedia for the same 1024 breakpoint, which renders 'mobile' on first paint then flips to 'desktop' after mount — a visible reflow of the campaigns table on desktop load. Worse, on Results both run simultaneously (the page's own listener AND the AgentPanel's hook), two parallel matchMedia subscriptions for one breakpoint. Same threshold, two implementations, one of which flashes.
issues.evidence: results-page.tsx:62-73 (mount-then-flip) and :134-135 (AgentPanel also uses useIsDesktop internally)

issues.title: Tablet Home wastes the screen — bento off, sidebar off, a lonely 640px column
issues.detail: The bento grid and the sidebar both gate at 1024, so on a 768-1023 iPad, Home is a centered 640px column with large empty margins on either side and no nav rail — neither the rich phone bottom-bar nor the desktop sidebar. It reads as a blown-up phone screen floating in whitespace, the classic 'stretched, not native' tablet failure. The 768 breakpoint raises padding and caps width but adds no tablet-specific structure.
issues.evidence: home.module.css:19-50 (640px cap @768, bento only @1024); globals.css:517 (sidebar only @1024)

issues.title: --header-h is a hardcoded 68px guess that every sticky offset depends on
issues.detail: The desktop sidebar, inbox sticky sheet, inbox empty placeholder, and dashboard rail all position with top: calc(var(--header-h,68px)+16px), but the actual sticky header has variable padding (14/16/18px top+bottom) so its real height shifts with breakpoint and the comment literally says 'approx ... (tune later)'. If the header is taller than 68px, every docked pane tucks slightly under it; if shorter, a gap opens. The whole two-pane vertical alignment rests on a magic number admitted to be untuned.
issues.evidence: globals.css:228 ('approx ... tune later'), :385,:532; inbox-design-base.css:216,235,244

opportunities.title: Make one nav component that re-flows across all three sizes
opportunities.detail: Collapse PrimaryNav and AppSidebar into a single source of truth that renders bottom-bar (phone), inline-header strip (tablet), and vertical rail (desktop) from the SAME items and the SAME editorial tokens (serif label, amber active accent). If desktop genuinely needs the extra Tools/Mira/Reports entries, add them as a quiet secondary group in the editorial voice rather than switching to lucide icons + shadcn surfaces. This kills the 'two apps' feeling in one move.

opportunities.title: Define a tokenized breakpoint ladder and a --bottom-nav-clearance var
opportunities.detail: Pick the canonical set (e.g. sm 560 / md 768 / lg 1024 / xl 1280) and document which transitions own which concern, then align the header inset and body grid to the SAME threshold (move both to 1024 or both to 1280) so the 1024-1279 limbo disappears. Add --bottom-nav-clearance and apply it once on .app-content below 768 so Home/Inbox/Results can never occlude their last row again.

opportunities.title: Unify the detail-surface model: one anchor, one breakpoint
opportunities.detail: Decide that 'detail' is a right-rail at >=1024 and a bottom-sheet below it for BOTH the agent panel and the inbox detail — driven by one shared useIsDesktop. That removes the tablet split where Home sheets slide up while Inbox sheets slide in. Then make the inbox right pane collapse to 0 when nothing is selected (list goes full-width; detail expands in only on selection) so the triage list owns the screen at rest.

opportunities.title: Give tablet (768-1023) real structure
opportunities.detail: Either pull the bento/two-pane down to 900px (tablets are wide enough for a 1fr+rail) or introduce a tablet-specific two-column Home so the iPad stops looking like a floating phone column. At minimum, surface the nav rail or keep the bottom bar through the tablet band instead of stranding it in a serif strip with no spatial anchor.

opportunities.title: Replace the magic --header-h with a measured value
opportunities.detail: Set --header-h from the real header via a ResizeObserver (or a CSS sticky/position-sticky layout that doesn't need the number) so every docked pane aligns exactly under the header at every breakpoint. Removes a class of subtle 'pane tucks under header' / 'gap above pane' bugs and the admitted 'tune later' debt.

topMoves: Unify navigation into ONE responsive component (bottom-bar / header-strip / rail) using the editorial token set, so desktop stops swapping in the generic shadcn icon-sidebar — the #1 source of the 'two different apps' feeling.
Establish a tokenized breakpoint ladder and align the header inset with the body-grid flip (both at 1024 or both at 1280) to erase the 1024-1279 limbo where chrome is desktop but insets are not.
Add a single --bottom-nav-clearance inset on .app-content below 768 (Inbox already needs 96px; Home/Results currently use 28px and occlude their last row).

Make the detail surface coherent: one shared 1024 breakpoint and one anchor (right-rail desktop / bottom-sheet mobile) for BOTH agent panel and inbox detail, and collapse the inbox right pane to 0 when empty so the triage list isn't permanently squeezed below 480px.
Replace the hardcoded --header-h:68px 'tune later' guess with a measured header height so every sticky docked pane aligns exactly across breakpoints.

surface: Marketing landing & first impression — src/app/(public)/welcome + (public)/layout.tsx + components/landing/v6/* (topbar, hero, synergy, beat-alex/riley/mira, control, pricing, closer, footer, dock, reveal, glyphs) + landing-v6.css
feelVerdict: This is the most confident, editorial surface in the whole product — a filmic "9-screen" scroll with mono corner-labels, a self-rotating agent headline, and two genuinely outstanding product proofs (Riley's live Meta-Ads dashboard with a real draft-approval card; Mira's brief→script→clip pipeline). It FEELS like a real company with taste. But the magic leaks at the two moments that matter most: the hero sells an abstract category ("a revenue desk you hire one seat at a time") instead of a felt outcome, and every "act now" path dead-ends in a mailto: email — so a sold medspa owner has to compose an email instead of clicking Start. Add a doubled-word typo on the marquee creative and zero social proof, and a premium frame is undercut by a hobbyist finish.

strengths.title: Riley's beat is the conversion engine — it shows the product working, not a feature list
strengths.detail: A full-bleed faux Meta-Ads dashboard: 5-step process loop (Plan→Launch→Optimize·now→Approve→Measure) with a live pulse on the current step, a 4-up stats strip ($3,184 spend / 87 leads / $36.60 CPL / 2.4x ROAS), a 5-row ad-set table that visually FLAGS the one saturated ad set (coral row tint + dot + 'Drafting pause' pill), and a Riley draft card with plain-English reasoning and Approve/Edit/Dismiss. This is exactly the 'governed, you-approve' value the whole company is about, made tangible in one screen. Best section by a mile.
strengths.evidence: beat-riley.tsx:96-298 (ROWS flag at :32-39, draft card :269-297, 'Illustrative example' disclaimer :301)

strengths.title: Honest-marketing guard tests enforce trust at the copy layer — rare and exactly right for this audience
strengths.detail: A vitest suite bans ~40 specific over-claims from ever shipping: fake SLAs ('twelve seconds', '12-second'), non-existent integrations (Cal.com, Notion), fictional agents (Sales Closer, Nurture Specialist), fake bundle discounts ('save 25%'), self-serve framing ('Free trial', 'Start free'), and deleted route hrefs. For a product asking owners to trust AI with their money, codifying 'we will not lie on the landing page' is a genuine strength most teams never build.

strengths.title: The agent-rotating hero headline is a strong, on-brand hook device
strengths.detail: Headline auto-cycles Alex/Riley/Mira every 3.2s with a 220ms crossfade ('Alex replies in seconds.' → 'Riley catches what you miss.' → 'Mira ships what you can't.'), coral-accenting only the verb. Rotation is correctly gated on hero-in-view via IntersectionObserver and STOPS the moment the user touches the toggle (userInteracted), with choice persisted to localStorage. It teaches the 3-agent model in the first 6 seconds without a wall of text.
strengths.evidence: agent-context.tsx:20-39 (heads), :81-90 (gated rotate), hero.tsx:65-73 (swap), agent-context.tsx:71-79 (interaction stop + persist)

strengths.title: Editorial 'numbered-screen' system gives the scroll a premium, intentional spine
strengths.detail: Every section carries a mono corner-label (01 Meet the desk … 09 Footer) plus a right-aligned slug ('Switchboard / always on', 'a 02 / · riley · paid spend'). Combined with General Sans + JetBrains Mono, hairline borders (hsl(20 8% 14% / 0.06)), text-balance headlines and clamp() type, it reads like a Stripe/Linear-tier narrative, not a template. Reveal-on-scroll (700ms, custom ease, IO-driven, fallback for above-fold, reduced-motion respected) adds craft without jank.

strengths.title: Control/safety section directly answers the #1 buyer fear ("will it spend without me?")
strengths.detail: An accordion of four guarantees — 'Agents draft. You publish.', 'Audited' (timestamp+agent+reasoning), 'Where your work lives' (connects to tools you already pay for, disconnect one click), 'Hands-off when ready' — keyboard-accessible (role=button, Enter/Space, focus-visible coral outline). The repeated 'you loosen the leash on your own time, not ours' is precisely the reassurance a non-technical owner needs.

issues.title: Conversion path dead-ends in a mailto: — there is no way to actually start, despite a real onboarding flow existing
issues.detail: Every primary CTA on the page (hero 'See Alex work', closer 'Start with Alex', dock 'Hire', all beat 'Start with X', topbar 'Get started') routes to the #pricing anchor. The pricing cards' final CTAs are then mailto:hello@switchboard.ai?subject=Start%20with%20Alex. So the entire funnel for a ready-to-buy operator is: scroll to pricing, then open their email client and compose a message. Meanwhile a real onboarding route (src/app/(auth)/onboarding) and register API (src/app/api/auth/register) already exist and are never linked. The only path into the app is a tiny 13px 'Sign in' link in the top-right. A time-poor medspa owner on a phone who is sold at the closer will not draft an email — this is the single biggest leak on the page and likely halves conversion versus a 'Book a 15-min setup call' / Calendly / typeform CTA.
issues.evidence: pricing.tsx:27/38/48 (mailto ctaHref), hero.tsx:84 + closer.tsx:48 (href={meta.anchor}→#pricing→mailto), topbar.tsx:58, dock.tsx:71; real routes unused: src/app/(auth)/onboarding, src/app/api/auth/register

issues.title: Doubled-word typo on the marquee creative — appears twice, in the showcase Mira beat
issues.detail: Mira's hero script reads: 'Three coffees. Two reds. One you you don't recognize in photos.' — 'you you' is a duplicated word, and the same error is repeated in the video-frame caption ('…you you don't recognize in photos.'). This is the single most-read piece of sample creative on the page (it's literally demonstrating Mira's copywriting quality), so a visible typo here actively undermines the 'Mira ships great creative' claim. A typo on a page selling AI-written marketing copy is uniquely damaging.
issues.evidence: beat-mira.tsx:129 and beat-mira.tsx:234 ('One you you don’t recognize' / '…you you don’t recognize')

issues.title: Zero credibility/social proof — every number is stamped "illustrative," and there is no human, logo, or testimonial anywhere
issues.detail: The page asks an owner to trust AI with ad spend and customer conversations, yet offers no trust anchors: no customer logos, no testimonial/quote, no founder or team, no 'X clinics run on Switchboard', no press, no security/compliance badge. Worse, the two data-rich proofs both carry honesty disclaimers ('Illustrative example. Actual numbers vary by account.' / 'Pilot pricing… may vary'). The guard-test discipline is admirable, but the net effect is a beautiful page where literally nothing is verifiably real. For a skeptical, money-on-the-line buyer, 'pretty but unproven' reads as risky. Even one real pilot quote or '12 CDMX clinics' stat would transform believability.
issues.evidence: beat-riley.tsx:301 ('Illustrative example…'), pricing.tsx:153-155 ('Pilot pricing…'); no logo/testimonial/founder component exists in components/landing/*

issues.title: Three-way visual-token fracture between landing, app layout, and legal pages — the brand doesn't hold together at the seams
issues.detail: The landing defines its own warm palette (--v6-cream hsl(28 30% 90%), --v6-coral hsl(14 75% 55%)) duplicated in both tailwind.config.ts:84-100 AND landing-v6.css:9-22. But the (public)/layout.tsx that wraps it paints a different, cooler base (--sw-base #F5F3F0) — so overscroll/rubber-band and any pre-hydration paint flashes a lighter color than the cream content, and the wrapper bg never matches. Then /privacy and /terms (LandingChrome → landing-nav/landing-footer) drop into a THIRD register entirely: inline-styled hex #1A1714 / rgba(249,248,246), .page-width, var(--font-display) Helvetica-ish nav — a visibly different product than the welcome page a click earlier. And the landing's coral action color (14 75% 55%) is not the in-app operator-amber (30 55% 46%), so a buyer who signs up sees a different accent hue than the one that sold them. Premium brands feel monolithic; this feels like three sites.
issues.evidence: tailwind.config.ts:84-100 vs landing-v6.css:9-22 (duplicated v6 tokens); globals.css:88 (--sw-base #F5F3F0) vs layout.tsx:25; landing-nav.tsx:30-53 (#1A1714 inline) used by landing-chrome.tsx for /privacy,/terms; coral hsl(14 75% 55%) ≠ in-app action hsl(30 55% 46%)

issues.title: The hero sells an abstract category, not a felt outcome — the first 5 seconds underperform the rest of the page
issues.detail: Above the fold the eyebrow says 'A revenue desk you hire one seat at a time', the rotating H1 says 'Alex replies in seconds.', and the sub is 'Hire one. Or hire the desk — they share context as they go.' 'Revenue desk' and 'share context' are insider/abstract framings; a medspa owner thinks in 'missed DMs', 'wasted ad spend', 'no time to post'. The genuinely visceral pain lines exist on the page — 'Leads die in twelve minutes', 'Your next ad has been almost-ready for two weeks' — but they're buried in beats 3 and 5, not in the hero. The strongest hook is 1500px below the fold. The first screen is elegant but makes the visitor work to translate 'desk' into 'this fixes my problem'.
issues.evidence: hero.tsx:61-63 (eyebrow), :75-78 (sub 'share context'); stronger pain lines stranded at beat-alex.tsx:74-75 and beat-mira.tsx:40-49

issues.title: Faux-interactive elements in the Riley dashboard set a trap for click — cursor-pointer buttons that do nothing
issues.detail: Riley's draft card renders 'Approve pause →', 'Edit allocation', and 'Dismiss' as <span> with cursor-pointer styling, and the Mira/Alex demos show typing indicators. On a touch device these read as tappable; an exploring owner WILL tap 'Approve pause' and nothing happens (no tooltip, no scroll-to-pricing, no 'this is a preview'). A demo that invites a tap and then ignores it feels slightly broken and erodes the 'this product is real and responsive' impression the dashboard works so hard to build. Either make them scroll to pricing/onboarding or visibly mark the frame 'preview'.
issues.evidence: beat-riley.tsx:286-294 (cursor-pointer spans, no onClick); beat-alex.tsx:140 ('typing…' static)

opportunities.title: Wire CTAs to a real first action and add a single sticky 'Start' affordance
opportunities.detail: Point the primary CTAs at the existing (auth)/onboarding flow (or a 'Book your 15-min setup' Calendly/typeform if pilot is still high-touch), keep mailto only as a tertiary 'or email us' link. Promote 'Get started' in the topbar and the dock 'Hire' button to that same destination so there's always one obvious next step in view. This is the highest-ROI change on the page — the funnel currently has no floor.

opportunities.title: Lead the hero with the pain, not the category
opportunities.detail: Swap the abstract eyebrow/H1 framing for the visceral lines already written elsewhere on the page. e.g. headline 'Stop losing leads while you sleep.' with the rotating agent verb as the second line, or an outcome sub like 'Replies in seconds · catches wasted spend · ships your next ad — you approve every move.' Keep the elegant rotation, just move the felt problem above the fold so the first 5 seconds hit as hard as beat 3.

opportunities.title: Add one honest credibility band — even a single real pilot quote or count
opportunities.detail: A thin strip under the hero or before pricing: one real operator testimonial (clinic name + city + photo), or a count ('Running for N CDMX clinics'), or a security line ('SOC2 in progress · your data never trains public models'). The guard tests keep it honest; the goal is one verifiable anchor so the page isn't 100% 'illustrative'. This directly attacks the 'pretty but unproven' risk for a money-on-the-line buyer.

opportunities.title: Unify the brand seams: make legal pages and the layout base match the landing palette, and reconcile coral↔amber
opportunities.detail: Re-skin landing-nav/landing-footer (used by /privacy,/terms) into the v6 cream/graphite register so a click away from the landing doesn't drop into a different-looking product; set (public)/layout.tsx background to --v6-cream so overscroll/initial-paint matches. Longer-term, decide whether the marketing coral and the in-app operator-amber should converge (or document the intentional marketing-vs-app split) so a buyer's accent color doesn't change the moment they sign in.

opportunities.title: Make the demo dashboards feel alive (and honest about being demos)
opportunities.detail: Either make Riley's Approve/Edit/Dismiss and the agent toggles scroll-to-onboarding on tap, or add a subtle 'Live preview' chip so the cursor-pointer affordances don't read as broken. A small, tasteful animation — the Riley draft card sliding in, or one stat ticking — on first reveal would make the single best section feel even more like a product caught mid-thought rather than a static screenshot.

topMoves: Fix the funnel floor: route every primary CTA to the real (auth)/onboarding flow (or a 'Book a 15-min setup' call), demote mailto to a tertiary option, and keep one persistent 'Start' affordance in the topbar/dock — today a sold buyer can only compose an email.

Rewrite the hero to lead with felt pain over abstract category: pull the already-written 'Leads die in twelve minutes' / 'almost-ready for two weeks' energy above the fold so the first 5 seconds hit as hard as the Riley/Mira beats.
Fix the doubled-word typo on Mira's showcase script ('One you you don't recognize', beat-mira.tsx:129 and :234) — a visible typo on the page selling AI-written copy is uniquely self-defeating.

Add one honest credibility anchor (a real pilot testimonial with clinic+city, or a 'running for N clinics' count, or a security line) so the page isn't 100% 'illustrative' for a money-on-the-line buyer.

Close the brand seams: re-skin /privacy & /terms (landing-nav/footer) and the (public) layout base into the v6 cream register, and reconcile the marketing coral vs in-app operator-amber so the brand feels monolithic from landing → signup.

surface: Onboarding & activation (first-run to value): login/page.tsx, post-auth sentinel, the 5-step (auth)/onboarding wizard (OnboardingEntry → TrainingShell → BusinessFactsStep → TestCenter → GoLive → LaunchSequence), supporting hooks (use-first-run, use-onboarding-draft, use-entrance-played), and the post-launch Home landing.
feelVerdict: The wizard's spine is genuinely strong — "paste your website and Alex drafts your business" is a real aha, the live playbook-fills-as-you-chat moment is delightful, and the readiness/escalation framing shows real care about trust. But the experience is calm-to-the-point-of-flat: it relies on system-font black buttons and muted-gray agent marks instead of the warm amber/identity palette the rest of the app uses, the "Go live anyway" path lets an operator unleash a money-spending, customer-messaging agent with zero tests, and the moment of arrival — the payoff for all that setup — dumps the new operator onto a Home screen that says "All caught up, 0 open enquiries" with no acknowledgment that they just launched. The first 2 minutes are competent and reassuring; they are not yet impressive or emotionally rewarding.

strengths.title: The aha is real and well-sequenced: scan → watch your playbook fill in
strengths.detail: The opening promise 'Paste your website and Alex will draft your services, hours, rules, and lead flow' is a concrete, high-value hook, and TrainingShell delivers it: it streams 'Looking at {url} now…' then 'I found {businessName}. I've filled in what I could' while PlaybookPanel visibly populates with a highlighted-section flash. This is a best-in-class onboarding pattern (show, don't ask) and is the single strongest moment in the slice.
strengths.evidence: onboarding-entry.tsx:75 ('Paste your website and Alex will draft…'); training-shell.tsx:34,67-71 (scan-start/scan-done messages); training-shell.tsx:111 (setHighlightedSection flash)

strengths.title: Trust is structurally front-loaded before any spend
strengths.detail: GoLive splits checks into 'Required to launch' (blocking) vs 'Recommended' (advisory) with green/red/amber iconography, the launch CTA is hard-disabled until isReady, and BusinessFactsStep explicitly asks 'Who should Alex hand off to when it can't handle a conversation?'. For a product asking an operator to let AI spend money, surfacing escalation + readiness gates before launch is exactly the right instinct.

strengths.title: Reassuring, low-pressure copy with reversibility baked in
strengths.detail: Copy repeatedly lowers the stakes: 'You can edit everything before going live', 'All fields are optional', 'Skip for now'. The 'Why this answer?' expander in TestCenter plus the 'Fix this' loop lets operators interrogate and correct Alex's reasoning, which builds confidence that the agent is editable rather than a black box.
strengths.evidence: onboarding-entry.tsx:95-96; business-facts-step.tsx:101; test-center.tsx:175 ('Why this answer?'); test-center.tsx:210-216 ('Fix this')

strengths.title: Draft is org-scoped and resumable across sessions
strengths.detail: useOnboardingDraft keys persistence on organizationId and the page resumes at playbookData.step, so an interrupted operator (mobile, time-poor) returns to where they left off rather than restarting. The wizard also clears the draft only on successful launch (handleLaunchComplete → clearDraft).
strengths.evidence: use-onboarding-draft.ts:11,28-36; onboarding/page.tsx:147 (step from server); onboarding/page.tsx:109-112 (clearDraft on complete)

issues.title: 'Go live anyway' lets an operator launch a money-spending, customer-messaging agent with ZERO tests
issues.detail: In TestCenter, if testedCount===0 the primary CTA shows a soft gate ('You haven't tested Alex yet') but still offers a fully-functional 'Go live anyway' button styled in muted gray — one click and the operator advances to launch. For the rest of the funnel the product is meticulous about trust (readiness gates, escalation contact), then at the one moment that proves the agent actually behaves correctly on THEIR data, it shrugs and lets them skip. A freshly-launched Alex's very first action could be a wrong price or a wrong booking to a real customer. This undercuts the entire trust thesis. The gate should at minimum require one passing scenario, or make 'skip' a deliberate, friction-ful confirmation ('I understand Alex will message real customers untested').
issues.evidence: test-center.tsx:40 (showZeroTestGate), test-center.tsx:281-287 ('Go live anyway' Button, backgroundColor var(--sw-text-muted))

issues.title: The payoff moment evaporates: launch dumps the operator onto an empty 'All caught up' Home with no 'you just launched' acknowledgment
issues.detail: handleLaunchComplete does router.push('/'), and for a brand-new org with zero decisions Home composes the CALM verdict: 'All caught up. Your team's running clean.' with proof '0 open enquiries'. After 5 steps of building and a launch animation, the operator's reward is a screen that reads as if a long-tenured team just finished a quiet day — not as if they just turned on their first AI employee. There is no 'Alex is now live and watching your WhatsApp', no 'here's what happens next', no first-run guidance. The emotional arc deflates exactly where it should peak, and a non-technical operator may think nothing actually happened.
issues.evidence: onboarding/page.tsx:109-112 (router.push('/')); home-page.tsx:222 (isCalm = decisionFeedAvailable && decisionCount===0); compose-verdict.ts:143 ('All caught up. Your team's running clean.')

issues.title: First-run scaffolding exists but is dead code — the post-activation guidance layer was built and never wired in
issues.detail: useFirstRun defines a full first-run model (bannerDismissed, reviewedConversations, sentTestLead, visitedPlaybook) gated on onboardingComplete===true, and useEntrancePlayed manages a one-time entrance animation flag. Neither has a single production consumer (grep finds only their own test files; useFirstRun's only sibling reference is a separate FirstRunNote in results/states.tsx that does not import the hook). So the intended 'welcome back, here are your first 3 things to do' activation checklist and the celebratory entrance simply do not render. The new operator gets zero post-launch orientation despite the plumbing existing.
issues.evidence: use-first-run.ts:19-42 (full model, no consumers); use-entrance-played.ts:15-28 (no consumers); grep: useEntrancePlayed/markPlayed referenced only in __tests__

issues.title: The wizard visually disowns the app's warm-editorial design language — wrong buttons, dead agent marks, system fonts
issues.detail: Three compounding inconsistencies make the wizard feel like a different (older, colder) product than the app it onboards into: (1) Primary CTAs use backgroundColor var(--sw-text-primary) — near-black — instead of the operator-amber that is the app's single action color, so 'Start scanning', 'Try Alex', 'Alex is ready. Go live' all read as generic black buttons; GoLive's final 'Launch Alex' DOES use --sw-accent, so even within onboarding the action color is inconsistent. (2) The AgentMark for Alex renders in muted gray (var(--sw-text-secondary, #6B6560)) with no agent identity hue, so the 'character' the operator is meant to bond with looks like a disabled gray glyph rather than a living teammate. (3) BusinessFactsStep wordmark is lowercase 'switchboard' with an inline sm AgentMark while every other step uses Capitalized 'Switchboard' display-font — a visible seam mid-wizard.
issues.evidence: onboarding-entry.tsx:104 & training-shell.tsx:328 & test-center.tsx:299 (backgroundColor var(--sw-text-primary)) vs go-live.tsx:241 (var(--sw-accent)); agent-mark.tsx:119 (color var(--sw-text-secondary,#6B6560)); business-facts-step.tsx:88-90 (lowercase 'switchboard')

issues.title: The 'real scenarios' test set silently degrades and the test responses are simulated against playbook, with no clarity on fidelity
issues.detail: TestCenter claims 'These scenarios use your actual services and rules', but generateTestPrompts returns [] entirely if services.length===0, and most prompts are conditional (pricing only if first.price, edge cases only if escalation triggers / hours exist). A thin playbook yields one lonely booking prompt or an empty prompt list with no explanation. Separately, the simulation calls /api/dashboard/simulate and the operator sees a polished chat with 'Why this answer?' annotations, but nothing communicates this is a sandbox/dry-run vs. how the live agent will behave — for a trust-critical moment, the boundary between 'simulated' and 'real' is invisible (the toolsAttempted.simulated flag exists in the response type but isn't surfaced).
issues.evidence: test-center.tsx:75 ('use your actual services and rules'); prompt-generator.ts:7 (returns [] if no services), prompt-generator.ts:19,28,44,53 (conditional prompts); use-simulation.ts:14-20 (simulated flag in type, unused in UI)

issues.title: Motion is inert across the wizard despite a rich keyframe library sitting unused
issues.detail: globals.css defines fade-in, aura-pulse-once, character-float, typing-dot, aura-breathe — a real motion vocabulary. Inside the wizard, only the typing dots and a 300ms launch-sequence opacity fade are used; steps mount with no entrance animation, the playbook 'fill' uses a 1500ms highlight but the panel itself doesn't animate in, and step transitions are instant cuts. The login mark breathes (animate-aura-breathe) but the onboarding marks are static. The result feels static and templated rather than 'alive', missing easy polish that competitors (Linear, Stripe) use to make setup feel premium.
issues.evidence: globals.css:441-463 (fade-in/aura-pulse/character-float utilities defined); onboarding/*.tsx (no animate-fade-in/animate-aura usage); training-shell.tsx:111-112 (highlight only, no panel entrance)

issues.title: Loading and error states across the wizard are bare strings, not designed states
issues.detail: The wizard's loading screen is the literal text 'Loading...' centered in muted gray; post-auth shows 'Signing you in…' as a lone paragraph with no spinner or brand mark; the scan-failure state is functional but minimal. For a first-ever impression, these unbranded fallbacks read as unfinished. A medspa owner's very first interaction after sign-in is a gray 'Signing you in…' with no logo, no motion, no warmth — the opposite of the 'capable, premium colleague' the product wants to project.
issues.evidence:
onboarding/page.tsx:130-138 ('Loading...'); post-auth/page.tsx:39 ('Signing you in…' bare

); training-shell.tsx:209 (scan error copy)

issues.title: No sense of progress, time-to-value, or 'where am I' across the 5 steps
issues.detail: There is no step indicator, progress bar, or 'Step 2 of 5' anywhere — each screen is a full-bleed view with a Switchboard wordmark and a contextual readiness label, but a time-poor operator can't see how much is left or how long this will take. The opening screen also makes no promise about effort ('this takes ~2 minutes'), so the operator commits blind. Best-in-class onboarding (Stripe, Mercury) always anchors expected effort and shows progress to reduce abandonment.
issues.evidence: onboarding/page.tsx:199-274 (switch with no shared progress chrome); onboarding-entry.tsx (no time estimate); training-shell.tsx:192-197 (readiness label is the only progress signal, and it's playbook-completeness, not wizard-step)

opportunities.title: Turn the launch moment into a real celebration that bridges into the live product
opportunities.detail: Replace the silent router.push('/') → empty CALM home with a first-run hero on Home for a freshly-launched org: 'Alex is live on WhatsApp and watching for your first lead' + a 3-item 'what happens next' checklist (Send yourself a test message · Review your first conversation · Adjust permissions). This is exactly what useFirstRun was built for (sentTestLead/reviewedConversations/visitedPlaybook) — wire the dead hook into Home and gate it on onboardingComplete && !bannerDismissed. Highest emotional ROI in the slice.

opportunities.title: Add a 'send a test message to your own phone' as the activation aha instead of/alongside in-app simulation
opportunities.detail: The strongest possible trust moment for a medspa owner is texting their own WhatsApp and watching Alex reply correctly in the real channel. After launch, prompt 'Text {connectedNumber} from your phone to see Alex in action.' This converts the abstract 'it's live' into a visceral, real proof and naturally exercises the live path. Pairs with surfacing the simulated/real boundary the current TestCenter blurs.

opportunities.title: Make the wizard look like the app it onboards into
opportunities.detail: Swap primary CTA backgrounds from var(--sw-text-primary) to operator-amber (--sw-accent) for one consistent action color, give the Alex AgentMark its identity hue (or the breathing aura it has on login) so the 'character' feels alive, and normalize the BusinessFactsStep wordmark to match the other steps. Low effort, high coherence payoff — the onboarding currently feels like a colder, older register than Home.

opportunities.title: Add a lightweight step rail + time-to-value promise
opportunities.detail: A persistent 'Train · Facts · Test · Launch' progress indicator (or a slim 1-of-5 bar) plus a '~2 minutes' anchor on the entry screen. Reduces abandonment for time-poor mobile operators and makes the multi-step commitment feel bounded and confident.

opportunities.title: Harden the test gate so launch implies 'I saw it work'
opportunities.detail: Require at least one scenario marked/sent before the standard 'Go live' CTA enables; convert 'Go live anyway' into an explicit, copy-heavy confirm ('Alex will message real customers without being tested — continue?'). Aligns the most trust-critical step with the rest of the funnel's care.
