opportunities.title: Breathe life into transitions with the existing keyframes
opportunities.detail: Apply animate-fade-in to each step's mount, animate the playbook panel rows in as they populate during the scan, and add a single aura-pulse on the Alex mark when a section gets filled. The vocabulary already exists in globals.css; using it would lift the whole flow from 'templated' to 'premium and alive' at near-zero cost.

opportunities.title: Design the loading/auth interstitials
opportunities.detail: Replace 'Loading...' and the bare 'Signing you in…' with a branded, breathing Switchboard/Alex mark and a one-line reassurance. First impression after sign-in should never be unstyled gray text.

topMoves: Wire the orphaned useFirstRun into a real post-launch first-run experience on Home: a celebratory 'Alex is live on {channel}' hero + a 3-step 'what happens next' checklist, so the activation payoff lands instead of dropping the operator onto an empty 'All caught up · 0 open enquiries' screen.

Close the 'Go live anyway' zero-test escape hatch: require at least one passing simulated scenario (or a deliberate, copy-heavy confirmation) before launch, so the product never lets an untested money-spending, customer-messaging agent go live one soft click away.

Re-skin the wizard to the app's warm-editorial language: amber primary CTAs (not near-black --sw-text-primary), an Alex AgentMark with identity hue/breathing aura instead of disabled-gray, and a normalized wordmark — so onboarding feels like the same premium product as Home.

Add a 'text Alex from your own phone' real-channel proof immediately after launch, turning the abstract 'it's live' into a visceral aha and exercising the live path the in-app simulation only approximates.

Add a slim step/progress rail + a '~2 minutes' effort promise, and replace bare 'Loading...'/'Signing you in…' interstitials with branded, motion-backed states using the already-defined fade-in/aura keyframes.

feelVerdict: Loaded with real work, this is the calmest, most confident screen in the app — a genuine editorial verdict, not a KPI wall. But with no loading skeleton it cold-starts into a FALLBACK that looks broken or asleep, it ships a permanently-empty "work in progress" module, and it has no cumulative "since you hired" trust anchor — so the calm tips into thin or anxious at exactly the moments that matter most.

strengths.title: The verdict hero is a point of view, not a stat grid
strengths.detail: A single 36→48px italic Newsreader sentence ('Two things need you — start with Alex.') over a mono eyebrow and a dot-led proof line. It leads with judgment, not metrics — exactly right for a time-poor operator who wants 'what needs me' in one read.

strengths.title: Disciplined honesty — never fabricates a number to look busy
strengths.detail: Every module degrades to a skeleton/quiet line; CALM only fires when the feed is genuinely live-and-zero, a feed error stays ACTIVE (no false all-clear), and the 'X of Y working' clause is dropped when roster is down. For a product trusted to spend money, this earned-trust discipline is load-bearing.

strengths.title: Per-module error isolation keeps Home calm under partial failure
strengths.detail: Each module is wrapped in HomeModuleBoundary, so one throwing section degrades to a muted 'This section is unavailable.' while siblings render — no full-screen error card on the editorial home. Correct premium failure posture.

strengths.title: Restrained, coherent tokens — amber=action, agent hue=identity only
strengths.detail: btnPrimary is amber (--action) only; agent color appears solely as the verdict em-accent, the team-pulse avatar, and a 3px quiet-row stripe. No identity color ever backs an action, and it avoids the hardcoded-hex 'T' drift of the cockpit family.

strengths.title: Needs-You is correctly scoped: ≤2 risk-gated cards with an honest overflow
strengths.detail: At most 2 live SwipeDecisionCards (financial/client-facing/high-risk can never be swipe-approved — they route to 'Tap to review'), a 'See all in Inbox →' only when more exist, and an Undo toast on approve. Home stays a triage launchpad, not a second Inbox.

issues.title: Cold-start paints the FALLBACK verdict + empty-state copy — looks broken/asleep, not loading
issues.detail: HomePage never branches on isLoading and there is no (auth)/loading.tsx, so first paint on a slow load is verdict FALLBACK 'Your team is on shift / we don't have a read on today yet' + 'All quiet overnight' + 'No active handoffs' + chips 'Not set up' — indistinguishable from a dead/empty account — which then snaps to real content as ~10 hooks resolve. The most-opened screen's first impression on a slow phone is 'is this even working?'.
issues.evidence: home-page.tsx (no isLoading; comment-only at :168); no (auth)/loading.tsx; compose-verdict.ts:86-94; home-page.tsx:198

issues.title: 'work in progress' is a permanently-empty, labeled module
issues.detail: workInProgressItems is hardcoded to [] (no WIP data source exists in P1-A), so the section ALWAYS renders header 'work in progress' + 'No active handoffs right now.' for every operator, every day, and on desktop sits permanently in the 320px rail. A labeled section that never has content reads as dead scaffolding and makes the team look idle.
issues.evidence: home-page.tsx:198,252-256; work-in-progress.tsx:26-37

issues.title: No cumulative 'since you hired' trust anchor — Home's longest lens is 7 days
issues.detail: The brief frames Home around cumulative 'since you hired' value, but that framing lives in the agent-panel Sheet, not Home — grep for 'since you hired'/'cumulative'/'hired' across src/components/home returns nothing. Home's only longitudinal note is ThisWeek ('Mon → today'), so on a quiet day there is no accumulated-value story and calm reads thin.
issues.evidence: grep 'since you|cumulative|hired' src/components/home/* → none; this-week.tsx:56

issues.title: Modules pop in independently — the screen settles rather than arrives
issues.detail: The ~10 hooks resolve on their own timelines with no coordination or transition: the verdict can flip FALLBACK→ACTIVE, chips flip 'Not set up'→live, and ThisWeek skeleton→prose at different moments, with the bento rail reflowing as it fills. Because the verdict shape depends only on the feed while its proof's 'X of Y working' depends on roster+state, the headline and its own proof line can visibly settle out of sync.

issues.title: Proof line can contradict the verdict ('0 open leads' under 'things need you')
issues.detail: buildActiveProof always emits '${openLeadCount} open leads' with no zero-guard, and decisionCount (decisions feed) and openLeadCount (greeting signal) are independent sources — so a real state is ACTIVE 'Two things need you' sitting directly above '0 open leads · …', undercutting the headline.
issues.evidence: compose-verdict.ts:51-59; home-page.tsx:88,98

issues.title: Number-word inconsistency: 'One'/'Two' spelled, '3 things' numeric
issues.detail: numberWord only spells out 2 ('Two'); the singular path hardcodes 'One'; 3+ fall through to digits, so the typeset hero mixes registers as the count grows ('One thing' / 'Two things' / '3 things'). In a deliberately editorial hero sentence, a bare digit reads cheaper than the spelled words around it.
issues.evidence: compose-verdict.ts:46-49,105-125

issues.title: Eyebrow timestamp is minute-precise but only updates on re-render — silently stale
issues.detail: formatEyebrow prints '… · 9:04 AM' to the minute at compose time with no ticking clock; data refetches every 60s but the verdict isn't recomposed on a timer, so the displayed minute drifts from reality. A precise wall-clock that's quietly wrong dents the 'live and on top of things' feeling.
issues.evidence: compose-verdict.ts:28-37; use-decision-feed.ts:25

issues.title: Font tokens diverge from the stated design system
issues.detail: The brief states Inter (body) + Source Serif 4 (editorial), but Home's editorial voice is --font-home-serif=Newsreader and --font-home-sans=Hanken Grotesk. Possibly intentional, but it's an undocumented per-surface divergence that risks the type voice splintering into several systems.
issues.evidence: globals.css:223-224; home.module.css:86

opportunities.title: Add a true loading skeleton for the verdict + modules
opportunities.detail: Give the verdict a dedicated 'loading' shape (a shimmering 2-line serif block + muted eyebrow) gated on the decision feed's pending state, and skeleton the chip ribbon and ThisWeek body, so first paint reads 'your briefing is loading' — never the FALLBACK 'we don't have a read on today' + 'All quiet overnight' combo. Cleanly distinguish three states: loading (shimmer) vs genuinely-calm (all-clear) vs feed-error (honest fallback).

opportunities.title: Replace the empty WIP module with a cumulative 'since you hired' value strip
opportunities.detail: Cut the permanently-empty 'work in progress' section and reclaim that quiet slot for the trust anchor the brief wants but Home lacks: 'Since you hired your team: 142 leads answered · 38 consults booked · ~$2,400 saved on ad spend.' It's the strongest reason a medspa owner is happy to open Home on a calm day, fills the rail with accumulated value instead of 'No active handoffs', and leans into the honesty discipline already present (only render fields you have).

opportunities.title: Coordinate the first composed paint so Home arrives, not assembles
opportunities.detail: Gate the verdict + main column on the small set of hooks that actually define the headline (decisionFeed, greetings, governance) reaching settled, then reveal the composed block in one soft transition rather than letting verdict shape, chips, and ThisWeek flip independently. Keep the quiet rail lazy. The operator should see a finished briefing fade in, never a headline contradicting its own proof mid-settle.

opportunities.title: Make the proof line self-consistent: drop zero/empty clauses
opportunities.detail: In buildActiveProof/buildCalmProof, omit any clause whose value is 0 or absent (no '0 open leads', no dangling separators) so the supporting line can only ever reinforce the verdict above it.

opportunities.title: Unify the hero number voice and de-precision the clock
opportunities.detail: Spell counts through ten in the verdict line (One/Two/Three/… things need you) for a consistent typeset voice, and drop minutes from the eyebrow (or tick it) so the timestamp never sits visibly stale. Small polish, but on the one sentence that earns the screen, register consistency separates premium from generic.

topMoves: Add a real loading skeleton (verdict shimmer + chip/ThisWeek skeletons) and split loading vs calm vs error, so cold-start never paints the 'we don't have a read on today / all quiet / no active handoffs' broken-looking combo.

Remove the permanently-empty 'work in progress' module and reclaim that slot for a cumulative 'since you hired: N leads answered · M consults booked · ~$X saved' value strip — the trust anchor Home currently lacks.
Coordinate the first composed paint so the briefing fades in finished, instead of verdict/chips/ThisWeek popping and reflowing independently as ~10 hooks settle.
Make the proof line self-consistent — drop zero/empty clauses so it can never read '0 open leads' under 'Two things need you'.
Unify the hero number voice (spell counts through ten) and drop the stale-prone minute from the eyebrow clock.

surface: Inbox — the core decision loop (src/app/(auth)/inbox/page.tsx → src/components/inbox/*, src/components/decisions/*, src/components/layout/inbox-drawer)
feelVerdict: The Inbox is genuinely beautiful and unusually calm for a decision queue — the editorial serif ledes, hairline risk pills, identity-only agent color, and the "card is a doorway" model are best-in-class restraint, and the handoff detail sheet is a standout (real lead context, conversation thread, suggested-opening prefill). But underneath the polish it does not yet FEEL trustworthy or fast where it counts: approving on the main page flies the card away but never refreshes the feed or the "things need you" count (stale until a 60s poll), the approval detail's entire "What this changes" panel is hardcoded em-dashes admitting "preview not yet wired," and the queue has no priority order, no batching, and zero keyboard model on the desktop two-pane. Right now it reads as a calm magazine you flip through, not a confident cockpit you clear — and for someone trusting it to spend their ad budget, the empty before/after and the stale count quietly undercut the trust the visuals work so hard to build.

strengths.title: The 'card is a doorway' model is exactly right for risk-gated triage
strengths.detail: Quiet chrome, a serif lede as the headline, no inline action buttons on the row — the real actions live in the detail sheet. Low-risk approvals can swipe-right to send; anything financial/client-facing/external/high rubber-bands back and routes to detail, never committing. This is the correct safety posture expressed as interaction, not a warning banner.
strengths.evidence: inbox-decision-card.tsx:30-44 (doorway doc + LOCKED RULES); swipe-policy.ts:8-10 canSwipeApprove; use-card-swipe.ts:201-208 (blocked right-swipe → primeBlocked, never commitApprove)

strengths.title: Risk is communicated in plain English, never as a traffic light
strengths.detail: Risk chips read 'Affects your ad spend or credits', 'Goes out to a client', 'No side effects outside Switchboard' instead of red/yellow/green dots. The risk pill is a hairline in ink/amber only. This is calm, literate, and respects a non-technical operator — a real differentiator vs typical dashboards.
strengths.evidence: risk-chips.ts:10-22; inbox-decision-card.css:365-396 (.risk-pill hairline, ink/amber palette, no fills)

strengths.title: The handoff detail sheet gives the operator genuinely everything to take over a lead
strengths.detail: Lead card (name, channel, service interest, phone/email, first-touch, qualification stage + score), 'Where it stands' (tone, turn count, topics, objections in quotes), a one-tap 'Start with this' suggested-opening prefill, the recent conversation thread with show-earlier, and a composer that states 'Sends to {name} on {channel} · {agent} stops replying'. This is best-in-class decision context.
strengths.evidence: handoff-detail-sheet.tsx:254-434; reason enum humanized in REASON_LABELS (e.g. 'Tricky objection','Tone turned') at handoff-detail-sheet.tsx:15-25

strengths.title: Failure states are honest and differentiated, not generic
strengths.detail: Reply delivery distinguishes 200 (handed back), 502 ('Saved — but we couldn't deliver it … reach out directly', draft kept) and true error ('nothing was saved … try again', draft kept). The list error never reuses the empty-state copy ('an error is an error, not all clear'). This restraint under failure is where trust is actually won.
strengths.evidence: handoff-detail-sheet.tsx:193-223,421-432; inbox-error-state.tsx:7-12 (regression-guard comment); inbox-screen.tsx:176-179 (isError BEFORE empty)

strengths.title: Undo is offered inline on every approve, with 409 handled silently
strengths.detail: Approve fires a toast with an Undo action; an already-terminal 409 returns { silent:true } and suppresses the toast so the operator never sees a confusing 'Approved' on a no-op. Reversibility-first is the right instinct for money-spending actions.

issues.title: Approving on the main Inbox page never refreshes the feed or the 'things need you' count
issues.detail: The full-page InboxScreen path commits via useRecommendationAction, whose onSuccess invalidates ONLY recommendations.all() and audit.all() — never decisions.feed(). The card's 280ms fly-off is purely local React state. So after you clear an item, the masthead count ('5 things need you'), the per-agent filter counts, and the underlying list do NOT update until the 60s refetchInterval or a navigation. Tellingly, the OLD header InboxDrawer path (dispatchDecisionAction) DOES invalidate decisions.feed() — so the primary surface has the worse behavior. The operator clears three items, the header still says 5, and either nothing visibly changes or items silently reappear/reorder on the next poll. This is the single biggest trust-and-momentum killer in the slice.
issues.evidence: use-recommendation-action.ts:36-42 (invalidates recommendations.all + audit.all only); query-keys.ts:98-101 vs 162-165 (recommendations vs decisions are disjoint key trees); dispatch-action.ts:88 (drawer path DOES invalidate decisions.feed); use-decision-feed.ts:26 refetchInterval 60_000

issues.title: The approval detail's 'What this changes' panel is entirely hardcoded em-dashes
issues.detail: For the highest-stakes decisions (financial/high-risk approvals that REQUIRE this sheet), the 'What this changes' section renders Before '—' → After '—', Confidence '—', Money at risk '—', with caption 'We're saving a slot here for live before-and-after numbers — wiring it up next week.' The operator is asked to approve spending THEIR money while the exact panel meant to show the impact openly admits it isn't built. The honest dashed treatment is well-intentioned, but on a confirm-gated money action it actively erodes confidence at the decision moment.
issues.evidence: approval-detail-sheet.tsx:203-235 (all values literal '—', 'preview not yet wired', 'wiring it up next week'); inbox-decision-card.css:714-804 (ds-pending dashed styling)

issues.title: The queue has no priority order and no batching — it's a flat, unsorted list
issues.detail: Every Decision carries urgencyScore and slaDeadlineAt, but inbox-screen.tsx renders decisions.map() in raw server order with no client sort and no grouping. The CSS even ships .queue-group-h styles for 'Needs you now / Can wait' rules — but no JSX ever emits them, so that design is dead code. There is no 'approve all low-risk', no swipe-batching, no near-SLA float-to-top. A time-poor owner with 12 items gets an undifferentiated stack and must read each lede to find what's urgent. Best-in-class inboxes (Superhuman, Linear) lead with what matters and let you clear safe items in bulk.
issues.evidence: inbox-screen.tsx:207-214 (decisions.map, no sort/group); types.ts:32 urgencyScore present but unused in UI; inbox.css:120-149 (.queue-group-h defined, never rendered)

issues.title: The desktop two-pane has no keyboard model — the core power-user flow is missing and the row is not accessible
issues.detail: The decision row is a <div className="decision-track" onClick> with no role, no tabIndex, no onKeyDown — only the agent avatar is a real button. So on the desktop two-pane you cannot Tab to a card, cannot Enter/Space to open it, and there is no j/k to move, Enter to open, or a/e to approve/skip. The only inbox keydown is Escape-to-close. A two-pane master-detail that you can only drive with a mouse is both a flow failure (the desktop tier exists precisely for fast triage) and a WCAG keyboard-operability failure for the primary action of the screen.
issues.evidence: inbox-decision-card.tsx:102-117 (div track, onClick only, no role/tabIndex/onKeyDown); inbox-screen.tsx:153-160 (only Escape); use-keyboard-shortcuts.ts (global = help/halt/escape only)

issues.title: Two parallel, visually divergent decision-card systems with different token conventions and different action wiring
issues.detail: The header InboxDrawer renders the OLD DecisionCard (inline Approve/Skip buttons, mapToDecisionCard, dispatchDecisionAction) while the full Inbox page renders the NEW InboxDecisionCard (doorway, useRecommendationAction). They even use different design-token systems: swipe-decision-card.module.css consumes shadcn raw-triples via hsl(var(--surface)), whereas inbox.css/inbox-design-base.css redeclare literal-hex tokens (--surface:#ffffff) scoped to .inbox-page/.sheet to dodge a documented collision. Same conceptual object, two looks, two code paths, two refresh behaviors — a maintenance and consistency hazard that will drift further, and a user who opens the drawer vs the page sees two different products.
issues.evidence: inbox-drawer.tsx:15-19,158 (DecisionCard + dispatchDecisionAction); inbox-decision-item.tsx:6,33 (InboxDecisionCard + useRecommendationAction); inbox-design-base.css:1-13 (token-collision note); swipe-decision-card.module.css:5-17 (two-convention note)

issues.title: The desktop empty-pane placeholder copy is flat and the whole detail rail starts empty on first load
issues.detail: On desktop ≥1024px, before anything is selected the right pane is a dashed box reading 'Select an item to see details' in italic serif — functional but generic, and it's the first thing a returning operator sees in the detail column every session. Combined with the unsorted list, the first-paint desktop impression is 'pick something, anything' rather than 'here's what needs you first.' A best-in-class two-pane auto-focuses the top item or previews the most urgent.
issues.evidence: inbox-screen.tsx:220-224; inbox-design-base.css:239-251 (.inbox-detail-empty dashed italic placeholder)

opportunities.title: Make the feed feel alive: optimistic-remove on commit + invalidate decisions.feed()
opportunities.detail: Add onMutate optimistic removal (and rollback on error) to useRecommendationAction, OR at minimum invalidate keys.decisions.all() in onSuccess so the masthead count and filter counts settle immediately after the fly-off. Mirror what dispatch-action.ts already does. This single change converts 'flip through a magazine' into 'watch the stack visibly shrink as I clear it' — the core momentum feeling.

opportunities.title: Sort the queue by urgency/SLA and ship the already-designed 'Needs you now / Can wait' groups
opportunities.detail: You already have urgencyScore, slaDeadlineAt, and the .queue-group-h CSS. Sort by SLA-soonest then urgencyScore, render the two group headers, and float near-SLA handoffs to the top. The data and styles exist — this is wiring, not design, and it's the difference between triage and a pile.

opportunities.title: Add a keyboard triage layer to the desktop two-pane
opportunities.detail: Make the card a focusable role=button (tabIndex 0, Enter/Space → open), then add j/k to move selection, Enter to open detail, and (only for canSwipeApprove items) a/e to approve/skip with the same fly-off + Undo toast. This is what makes Superhuman/Linear feel effortless and also fixes the accessibility gap for the screen's primary action.

opportunities.title: Replace the dashed 'What this changes' placeholder with whatever real signal exists today, and hide the rest
opportunities.detail: Until before/after is wired, render the concrete facts you DO have on confirm-gated approvals (e.g. the risk chips already say 'Affects your ad spend or credits' — pair that with any amount/budget the recommendation carries), and drop the empty Before/After/Confidence/Money grid rather than showing four em-dashes labeled 'preview not yet wired' at the exact moment money is approved. An honest absence is better as silence than as a broken-looking grid.

opportunities.title: Add a 'clear the safe ones' bulk affordance
opportunities.detail: Surface a quiet 'Approve N low-risk' action (only for items where canSwipeApprove is true) so an owner can clear the no-side-effect items in one confident gesture and spend their attention on the financial/client-facing ones. Pair with a single grouped Undo. This is the 'satisfying to clear the inbox' moment the slice is missing.

opportunities.title: Auto-select the top item on desktop and give the empty rail a warmer, more directive first paint
opportunities.detail: On ≥1024px with a non-empty queue, preselect the most urgent decision into the right pane so the operator lands on a real decision, not 'Select an item.' When truly empty, echo the calm 'That's everything' tone rather than the generic dashed placeholder.

topMoves: Wire feed freshness: optimistic-remove on commit and invalidate decisions.feed() in useRecommendationAction so the 'things need you' count and list shrink the instant a card flies off (today they go stale for up to 60s — the #1 trust/momentum bug).

Replace the hardcoded em-dash 'What this changes' panel on approvals: show the real risk/amount signal you already have and hide the empty before/after grid, so a money-spending confirmation never reads 'preview not yet wired' at the decision moment.

Give the queue a spine: sort by SLA/urgencyScore and render the already-styled 'Needs you now / Can wait' groups so urgent items lead — turning a flat pile into real triage with the data and CSS that already exist.

Add a keyboard model to the desktop two-pane (focusable cards, j/k navigate, Enter open, a/e approve-skip) — fixes both the missing power-user flow and the WCAG keyboard-operability gap on the screen's primary action.

Converge the two decision-card systems (header InboxDrawer's old DecisionCard vs the page's InboxDecisionCard) onto one card, one token convention, and one refresh path to kill the consistency/maintenance drift and the two-different-products feel.

surface: Agent cockpits — Alex / Riley / Mira identity, trust & the creative desk (src/app/(auth)/{alex,riley,mira}, src/components/cockpit/*, src/components/agent-panel/*)
feelVerdict: Two products wearing one badge. The Alex/Riley read-only AgentPanel is genuinely excellent — honest, calm, warm-editorial, first-person, with airtight loading/error/empty discipline; it feels like a trustworthy colleague's status card. But the moment you cross into Mira's creative desk you land in a colder, monospace-labeled, serif-less inline-styled surface that reads more like a config panel, and the carefully hand-built "alive" agent (1,100+ lines of animated pixel sprites, per-state animation mappers) is effectively invisible to the live operator — agents never move, and the same agent wears two different faces on adjacent screens. The trust scaffolding is real and impressive; the spark of a living personality has been quietly disconnected.

strengths.title: The AgentPanel honest-body composition is a masterclass in trustworthy status design
strengths.detail: Five explicit slots (identity status → key-result hero → open decisions → work log → freshness foot) each own their own loading/error/empty branch so one slot's failure never blanks the others. Error states refuse to lie ('Couldn't load decisions', never '0 / nothing waiting'); zero is shown dimmed-but-present (heroZero) and explicitly distinguished from error. This is exactly the reversibility-and-honesty posture a medspa owner trusting AI with their money needs.
strengths.evidence: agent-panel/open-decisions.tsx:42-61 (error vs empty split), key-result.tsx:44-50 (loading guard with three-states comment), agent-panel.module.css:338-347 (.heroZero 'zero ≠ error')

strengths.title: First-person work-log voice makes Alex feel like a colleague reporting in, not a log
strengths.detail: composeActivityVoice renders 'I replied to Maya R.…', 'I booked Maya's consult…', 'I escalated Maya to you —…'. The header is deliberately honest about what it can't know ('handled N things recently' rather than inventing 'since you last looked' because no last-viewed timestamp exists). This restraint is what separates premium from gimmicky.

strengths.title: The cumulative 'since you hired Alex' hero reframes the agent as an investment, not a feature
strengths.detail: The key-result hero leads with a 56px serif numeral (var(--font-home-serif), tnum) eyebrowed 'since you hired Alex', falling back to 'this week' only when lifetime is unavailable — and binds the eyebrow strictly to whichever window actually returned, so the number is never mislabeled. Riley's CPL comparator stays neutral-ink 'over/under your target' with no green/red sentiment. Calm, premium, anxiety-free.

strengths.title: Mira's draft-only safety contract is repeated everywhere it matters, and reversibility is honored
strengths.detail: Every Mira surface restates 'Draft only — not published. Nothing goes live without you.' Continue is a cost-confirm gate (mutation never fires before explicit Confirm, with a live $ estimate), Stop is flagged 'Irreversible' with a dotted red marker and a free/can't-be-undone explainer, and Keep is reversible via a quiet 'Un-keep'. For a surface that spends real provider money, this is the right amount of friction in the right places.

strengths.title: Mira's brief box kills the blank-box freeze and refuses off-scope requests in character
strengths.detail: The 'What should Mira work on next?' brief offers example chips ('Summer Botox special — $11/unit through July'), Goal/Vibe chips, an Intent-Preview readback that doubles as the cost-confirm, and an off-scope redirect that never answers a scheduling/results question but points back to ad creative. This is a genuinely product-grade conversational affordance.

issues.title: The agents never come alive — the entire animated-sprite system is disconnected from every live surface
issues.detail: There are 1,123 lines of hand-built pixel-sprite art (alex-variants 639, riley-variants 484) plus a full frame-cycling engine (useFrameCycle, AnimatedSprite) and per-agent animState() mappers that translate WORKING/WATCHING/REVIEWING into 'draft'/'sleep'/'idle' animations. Yet on the live path: TeamPulse on Home renders a flat CSS letter monogram; the AgentPanel header renders InboxAgentAvatar with a hardcoded state='idle' (never animating); and the ONLY component that ever requests an animated state (ApprovalCard, state='draft') is dead code — it is imported solely by roi-bar.tsx, which is itself imported by nothing live. So a busy owner opening Alex's panel sees a frozen 44px chip, not a colleague who looks like they're working. The single biggest 'is this thing alive and on my side' signal was built and then unplugged.
issues.evidence: inbox-agent-avatar.tsx:53-61 (state='idle' hardcoded); team-pulse.tsx:38-40 ({name[0]} letter, no sprite); approval-card.tsx:69 (state='draft') with grep showing its only importer is the also-dead roi-bar.tsx; alex-config.ts:31-35 animState unused on live path

issues.title: The same agent wears two different faces on adjacent screens
issues.detail: On Home, an agent's avatar is a serif-italic letter on a solid agent-hue disc (--agent-alex, 30px, font-home-serif italic). One tap later in the AgentPanel header, the same agent's avatar is a pixel-sprite chip on an accentSoft square (InboxAgentAvatar, 44px, hardcoded hex accents like #F4D5C5). Two visual identities, two color sources (HSL token vs hex), two shapes (circle vs rounded-square) for the literal same entity, within one gesture. Identity should be the most stable thing in the product; here it flickers between registers and undermines the 'I know who this is' confidence.
issues.evidence: home.module.css:271-291 (.agentChipAv serif-italic circle, hsl(var(--agent-alex))); inbox-agent-avatar.tsx:24-33 (sprite chip, MIRA_ACCENT hex #E7E1F0/#4A3A66) used at agent-panel.tsx:93

issues.title: Mira's creative desk reads as a different (colder) product than the rest of the app
issues.detail: The whole Mira family (desk, brief, trays, shelf, detail, feed) is built with inline styles off the hex-'T' token object and JetBrains-Mono uppercase eyebrows on nearly every section ('IN PRODUCTION', 'READY TO REVIEW', 'KEPT DRAFTS', 'PROVIDER COST'), with NO Source-Serif display anywhere — while Home and the AgentPanel speak warm HSL tokens and serif display heroes. The result: Alex/Riley feel editorial and human; Mira — the agent whose entire job is taste and warmth — feels like an engineering console. Her big number is a 36px sans 'count', not the 56px serif hero Alex gets. The creative director has the least creative-feeling surface in the app.
issues.evidence: mira-desk-page.tsx:42 (background:T.bg inline); mira-ready-to-review.tsx:24-46 (JetBrains-Mono eyebrow + 36px sans count) vs key-result hero serif 56px (agent-panel.module.css:315); mira-in-production-tray.tsx:22-32 + mira-kept-shelf.tsx:24-34 (mono uppercase headers)

issues.title: Riley has almost no distinct character versus Alex on anything an operator actually sees
issues.detail: riley-config.ts defines a teal identity, WATCHING/REVIEWING states, a 'REVIEWING is the only state that pulses' rule, a composer placeholder, and a command catalog — but the file's own comments admit none of it is wired: the StatusPill hard-imports Alex's statusColor/statusPulse, and Riley 'renders Alex's pill colors at runtime.' On the live panel Riley is the same static avatar + same four slots + same voice as Alex, differentiated only by hue and the role label 'Ad optimizer.' The ad-spend agent — the one literally authorized to burn money — should feel the most distinct and accountable, and instead feels like Alex reskinned.
issues.evidence: riley-config.ts:14-44 (comments: 'NOT wired in B.1', 'Riley renders Alex's pill colors at runtime'); RILEY_COMMANDS:58-66 ('Catalog ships as typed data only … wires later'); agent-display.ts:6-10 (role-label-only differentiation)

issues.title: Mira's drill-in from the AgentPanel is a dead-end stub while Alex/Riley get a full briefing
issues.detail: Open Alex or Riley from Home and you get the rich 4-slot honest body. Open Mira and you get a centered 'M' disc, 'Mira is set up', one sentence, and an 'Open Mira's workspace →' button — no status, no key result, no recent work, no open decisions. So the panel's implicit promise ('tap any teammate to see what they're up to') silently breaks for one of the three. It reads as 'Mira is a second-class citizen,' which erodes the team metaphor the surface is selling.
issues.evidence: mira-panel.tsx:16-30 (enabled branch is just heading + sub + CTA); contrast agent-panel.tsx:103-116 (Alex/Riley four-slot body)

issues.title: The agent halt control uses a saturated red/green as its action color, against the product's own amber doctrine
issues.detail: The cockpit Identity header's Halt/Resume button paints its label T.red (#A03A2E) for 'Halt' and T.green (#3F7A36) for 'Resume' — a high-stakes, color-coded stop/go control in a system whose stated discipline is 'operator-amber is the single action color; identity hues and sentiment colors are identity/status only.' A red 'Halt' button on Mira's desk also collides emotionally with the red 'Irreversible/Stop' language elsewhere on the same surface, muddying which red means 'destructive' versus 'pause.' (This control is also one of the few remaining live uses of the hex-T identity component, via the Mira desk/feed.)
issues.evidence: identity.tsx:163 (color: halted ? T.green : T.red on the halt button); tokens.ts:15-16 (green/red); collides with creative-detail-page.tsx:226 red Stop

issues.title: Loose 'Loading…' / plain-text fallbacks on Mira surfaces undercut the polish the panel sets
issues.detail: The AgentPanel uses pulsing skeletons and italic-serif empty lines. The Mira desk and detail page fall back to bare text: 'Loading Mira's desk…', 'Loading draft…', a #000 rectangle for the feed skeleton, and grey 'No drafts to review yet' on black. Functional, but next to the panel's considered loading language it reads unfinished and lowers the perceived craft of the agent whose job is craft.
issues.evidence: mira-desk-page.tsx:69 ('Loading Mira's desk…'); creative-detail-page.tsx:19 ('Loading draft…'); mira-creative-feed.tsx:48-49 (bare #000 skeleton)

issues.title: The AgentPanel freshness stamp is render-time, not data-time, so it can claim freshness that isn't
issues.detail: The 'as of {time}' foot is computed from new Date() at render (the code comments justify this as 'provenance over liveness' because the four slots don't share an asOf). But to an operator 'as of 3:42pm' reads as 'this data is current to 3:42pm' — when in fact the underlying queries may be minutes stale from cache. It's a small honesty gap on a surface whose entire thesis is honesty.
issues.evidence: agent-panel.tsx:77-86 (clockLabel from render-time new Date(), comment acknowledges no data asOf)

opportunities.title: Switch the agents on: wire animState into the live AgentPanel header and TeamPulse
opportunities.detail: The hard part (sprite art, engine, per-state mappers) is already built and tested. Replace InboxAgentAvatar's hardcoded state='idle' with a state derived from the agent's real status (busy/idle/halted → draft/idle/sleep), and give TeamPulse the same sprite instead of a CSS letter. A medspa owner glancing at Home should see Alex subtly 'working' when there's an open lead and 'sleeping' when halted. This single change converts 1,100+ lines of dormant craft into the product's strongest aliveness signal at near-zero new-build cost.

opportunities.title: Unify the agent avatar into one component and one token source
opportunities.detail: Pick the sprite-chip (it's the richer, more characterful artifact) and render it everywhere an agent appears — Home TeamPulse, AgentPanel header, Inbox rows, Mira desk identity — driving its accent from the HSL --agent-* tokens rather than the hex MIRA_ACCENT/RILEY_ACCENT hardcodes. One face per agent, one color system, zero cross-surface flicker.

opportunities.title: Re-skin Mira's desk into the warm-editorial register so the creative director feels creative
opportunities.detail: Port the Mira family from inline hex-T styles onto the globals.css token system used by the panel: give 'Ready to review' a serif hero number, replace the wall of JetBrains-Mono uppercase eyebrows with the panel's quieter sans/serif section treatment, adopt the skeleton/empty-line loading language, and let Mira's violet identity warm the surface (identity-only) the way coral/teal warm Alex/Riley. The goal: walking from Alex's panel into Mira's desk should feel like the same building, not a different app.

opportunities.title: Give Mira a real drill-in body in the AgentPanel
opportunities.detail: Replace the 'Mira is set up → Open workspace' stub with an honest mini-briefing: drafts ready to review (count), what's in production, last creative shipped, and the same freshness foot — mirroring the Alex/Riley slot rhythm. Keep 'Open workspace →' as the footer CTA. This closes the team-metaphor gap so all three teammates answer the same question when tapped.

opportunities.title: Differentiate Riley's competence cues from Alex's, and recolor the halt control to amber
opportunities.detail: Wire Riley's pill colors/pulse (the WATCHING/REVIEWING rule already exists) so his presence reads differently from Alex's, and surface one Riley-specific accountability line in the panel (e.g. spend pace vs guardrail) so the money-spending agent visibly carries more weight. Separately, repaint the Halt/Resume control to the single operator-amber (or a neutral ink outline) so 'pause' stops competing with 'destructive/irreversible' red and the app's one-action-color doctrine holds on every agent surface.

opportunities.title: Delete or quarantine the dead cockpit family to stop two-token drift at the source
opportunities.detail: ApprovalCard, roi-bar, kpi-strip, kpi-tile and their hex-T styling are orphaned from the live tree (verified: ApprovalCard's only importer is the unused roi-bar). Removing or clearly fencing them shrinks the hex-'T' footprint to just what Mira still needs, makes the 'one token system' migration tractable, and removes the trap where a future change reskins a screen no operator can see.

topMoves: Switch the agents on: derive the AgentPanel header sprite (and TeamPulse chip) from real agent status using the already-built animState/sprite engine, so Alex/Riley visibly 'work' and 'sleep' instead of sitting as frozen idle chips — the highest-leverage aliveness win in the slice and almost entirely a wiring job.

Unify agent identity to one avatar component and one color source (HSL --agent-* tokens) across Home, AgentPanel, Inbox and Mira, eliminating the serif-letter-circle vs pixel-sprite-square split that makes the same agent look like two entities one tap apart.

Re-skin Mira's creative desk into the warm-editorial register (serif hero number, fewer monospace eyebrows, panel-grade loading/empty states, violet-as-identity warmth) so the taste-and-warmth agent stops feeling like an engineering console next to Alex/Riley.
Give Mira a real four-slot drill-in body in the AgentPanel instead of the 'Open workspace' stub, so all three teammates honor the same 'tap to see what they're up to' promise.

Recolor the Halt/Resume control to the single operator-amber (retiring the saturated red/green stop-go), and reserve red strictly for irreversible/destructive Mira actions, restoring the product's one-action-color discipline on every agent surface.
